An accountant who has passed an official examination and fulfilled other requirements and has thus been licensed as an accountant by his or her state. ABBRV. CPA.
To confirm formally; to authenticate in writing. N. certification.

A writ issued by an appellate court to a lower court requesting the official record of a decision made by the lower court so that the appellate court can review it for errors. cestui que. (French) The one who.
The person who has a beneficial interest in a trust; the beneficiary.
The person who has a right to use property held by another.
The person whose life determines the duration of a trust or insurance contract.
Computer Fraud and Abuse Act.
The history of ownership of a property, listed from the original owner to the present one.
To object; to dispute the truth of a statement. N. (1) An objection. (2) An objection by a lawyer to the inclusion on the jury of a juror proposed by opposing counsel. challenge,
An objection to a juror based on a specific reason, which the judge must approve.
A hall or room in which a judicial or legislative body meets.
A judge's private office.
An illegal agreement between a party to a lawsuit and another person who agrees to pay the costs of pursuing the lawsuit in exchange for a share of the resulting proceeds.
(1) A senior officer, especially at a university. (2) The judge of a court of chancery. (3) In some European countries, the head of the government.
Equity, or equitable jurisdiction. Court of chancery. A court of equity.
To alter or modify; to substitute one thing for another,
Business reorganization in which a court supervises an insolvent business while it continues to operate and comes up with a plan for reorganization. bankruptcy,
A proceeding to allow for family farmers and fisherman to create a repayment plan to pay off debts over a specified period of time while being supervised by the court.
(French) Bench; the place where a court sits; the full court with all judges. See also en banc.
A proceeding that lets a borrower with enough income pay off bills over a specified period of time while being supervised by the court.
Straight bankruptcy; a proceeding that liquidates property, pays off debts, and leaves the debtor discharged. bankruptcy,
A person's moral qualities; reputation; disposition.
Evidence about a person's reputation in society.
A witness who attests to a person's reputation and moral qualities.

(1) To accuse someone of an offense. (2) To entrust someone with a duty. (3) To request money for something purchased or provided. (4) To issue instructions to a jury as to how they should analyze the facts and arguments presented at trial. N. charge.
A trust created to benefit a class or the public for educational, charitable, scientific, or religious purposes. trust,
Donations of money or other valuable items to charitable organizations; often tax-deductible.
Under the Internal Revenue Code, a corporation or business devoted to specific charitable purposes and not operated for profit or political influence.
A trust whose property must be used for charitable purposes.
(1) An organization whose focus is on charitable work and public benevolence. (2) The donation of money or help to those in need. charity,

A document granted by the state permitting the creation of a corporation, city, or university and defining its rights and privileges. See also articles of incorporation. V. To hire an airplane, boat, or other such vehicle for private use.
A possession; movable or personal property, as opposed to land or real property; something that can be owned.
A mortgage placed on personal property to secure a debt; chattel mortgages have been mostly replaced by the Uniform Commercial Code's security arrangements.
A document that records a debt and a security interest or lease of specific goods.
Examine;

A scam in which an individual deposits worthless checks in different banks and uses his or her good credit to withdraw funds before the checks clear, attempting to deposit the money into the account on which the check is drawn before the discrepancy is discovered. See also bad check.
An international agreement effective in 1997 that prohibits its signatories from creating and stockpiling chemical weapons such as nerve gases.
Most important;
The judge who presides over a court with more than one judge.
(1) Offspring, a son or daughter. (2) A person under the age of majority; a minor. PL. children.
Physical, sexual, or mental mistreatment of a child.
Laws regulating the hours, conditions, and nature of work that may be performed by children.
Financial contributions that a parent must make toward the upbringing of a child, usually paid by the parent who does not keep custody of the child after a divorce.
A federal law enacted in 2000 that requires schools and libraries that receive federal funding to
To inhibit; to discourage.
Limits on constitutional rights, such as free speech, created by fear of repercussions rather than outright government prohibition.
A policy used by securities firms that prevents all departments from accessing nonpublic information in order to prevent insider trading and self-dealing.
Completed. See also inchoate.
A lien that has been perfected and can be enforced.
(French) A thing; a personal possession.
A claim that can be brought as a lawsuit.
Something that is owned or can be owned.
Excessive and unnecessary trades of stock by a broker in order to increase his or her commission.
Cost, insurance, and freight. Also called C.F.I.
Children's Internet Protection Act.
A judicial division of a state or the country, usually covering several counties or districts.
A court that hears cases from within a judicial circuit, so-called because its judges traveled a circuit within the region, hearing cases in different places. See also district court.
discreet. See also abuse of discretion. discretion,
Evidence drawn from inference or deduction; secondary evidence. See also direct evidence, indirect evidence.

(1) A reference in a legal document or argument to a legal authority such as a precedent or statute. (2) A summons issued by a court ordering its recipient to appear in court at a specified date and time. V. cite.
A legally recognized member of a nation or state, who owes allegiance to the government and in return receives the government's protection.
The status of a citizen. See also naturalize.
A court that hears civil cases. court,
A lawsuit brought by a private citizen to protect a private or civil right or to seek a civil remedy; a noncriminal action.
A court handling civil actions, i.e., noncriminal matters.
Deliberately refusing to obey a law as a way of protesting its unfairness.
(1) Law concerned with citizens and private matters. (2) The body of jurisprudence created by a nation or state, as opposed to natural law or international law. (3) A system of

Personal rights and immunities from government oppression established and guaranteed by the Constitution, including freedom of speech and freedom of association; natural liberties that cannot be limited by the government. See also civil rights.
The laws governing procedure and practice used in civil litigation.

The rights of all citizens to personal liberties, freedom, and equality; rights specifically granted through laws enacted by communities, as opposed to civil liberties, which are rights that the government is not allowed to restrict.
Federal laws enacted to protect personal rights guaranteed by the Constitution, particularly freedom from discrimination.
The professional staff and workers of a government.
A commission that exists to ensure that civil service jobs are awarded on the basis of merit rather than favoritism.
A person not in the military; a private citizen.

(1) To state the truth of something; to assert. (2) To assert ownership of or right to money or property and demand it. N. (1) A demand for something that one considers one's own. (2) An application for compensation filed with an insurer. (3) A cause of action, including a right enforceable by the court, an injury, and damages.

(1) In property, the claim that someone in adverse possession of land intends to claim the land as his or her own. (2) In taxation, the requirement that a taxpayer report all income for the year, even if some of it might have to be repaid in a later year. Claims Court, U.S. See United States Court of Federal Claims.
One who makes a claim.
A group of people or things sharing common characteristics.
A lawsuit brought by one or a few members of a group on behalf of all the members, specially certified by the court to confirm that all the members share a concern and meet other requirements.
Common stock divided into classes.
To put into groups on the basis of similarities and shared characteristics. N. classification.
A paragraph, stipulation, or article in a legal document such as a contract or pleading.
A federal statute that amends the Sherman Antitrust Act by prohibiting price discrimination and regulating mergers and contracts that might inhibit competition.
Federal and state statutes that regulate air pollution.

A doctrine holding that in order for someone to receive equitable relief for a wrong, he or she must be blameless in the transaction, and must have performed his or her duties in good faith without wrongdoing. See also unclean hands.
Obvious or evident; easy to understand; without a doubt.
A constitutional doctrine that allows the government to restrict freedom of speech in cases where free speech would create an obvious and immediate danger that the government has a duty to prevent.
A title free of encumbrances; good title.
A banking establishment where member banks exchange checks and bills all at once, paying only the balances in cash so as to settle daily accounts conveniently.
Highly probable, establishing a firm belief that something is true; an evidentiary standard between a preponderance of the evidence and beyond a reasonable doubt.
Mercy, forgiveness, or leniency.
Religious professionals; those ordained for the ministry.
A member of the clergy.
Having to do with bookkeeping, records, filing, correspondence, and similar duties.
A mistake made while drafting or copying a document.

(1) A person who works in an office handling documents, records, and other administrative duties. (2) A person who works in a store, selling and organizing merchandise, usually an assistant. (3) A court officer who keeps court records, files pleadings and motions, issues process, and enters judgment. V. To work as a clerk, especially a law clerk. clerk,
The period spent working as a law clerk.
A contract formed when a user clicks a box on a website agreeing to the website's terms and conditions; see also browse-wrap agreement, shrink-wrap agreement.

An individual or organization that employs a professional to provide that professional's services; someone who employs an attorney to represent him or her in court, to draft legal documents, to advise, or to provide other legal services.

A corporation whose shares are not publicly traded but instead are held by a small group or a single shareholder who also runs the corporation's business; also called a closely held corporation. corporation,
A business in which a collective bargaining agreement requires that all employees be members of a particular union. See also open shop.
The conclusion of a sale of real estate in which the buyer pays the purchase price, the mortgage is secured, and the seller hands over the deed to the buyer.
An attorney's final arguments to the judge and jury at the conclusion of his or her case, summing up the facts supporting the case and explaining why the opposing side's case is inadequate.
Expenses that must be paid on closing, including fees for title searches and title insurance, deeds, appraisals, credit reports, and escrow.
A document that records the details of the sale, including purchase price, deductions, tax adjustments, and credits, resulting in the net amount that the seller receives.
An encumbrance or claim on a title that could potentially impair the title but can be proven invalid by outside evidence, and can be removed by an action to quiet title.
A vote by a legislature to end excessive debate on the matter at hand and vote on it. See also filibuster.
Someone who is a defendant together with someone else accused of the same crime or offense, or who is sued with another defendant in a single action.
One of two or more heirs inheriting from the same estate.
Consolidated Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act of 1985.
Pleading done according to the requirements of a procedural code. See also Federal Rules of Civil Procedure.
The collection of laws that govern the administration of military justice for all the armed services of the United States.
A compilation of guidelines for the professional conduct and ethical behavior of attorneys and legal professionals; has been replaced by the Model Rules of Professional Conduct.
An addendum to a will, modifying, adding to, revoking, or explaining portions of the original document.
To arrange laws or rules systematically. N. codification.
To force someone to act against his or her wishes, through the use of verbal or physical threats or other forms of compulsion. N. coercion.
Within a court's jurisdiction.
Jurisdiction or judicial notice.
A debtor's written confession of judgment in the event of nonpayment.
A promissory note written by a debtor authorizing an attorney to confess judgment against him or her if the debt described in the note is not paid when due.
To live together; to share a dwelling as a married couple without being married. N. cohabitation.
A headdress; a white piece of cloth worn by top English lawyers on top of their wigs. Coif,
A form of insurance in which the insurer pays only a portion of a claim for damage, sharing the risk with the insured.

(1) To collect information and arrange it in order. (2) When someone dies intestate, to estimate the value of all advances made by the deceased to his or her children before death so that these advances can be considered in dividing the estate according to law. N. collation.
An heir who is not directly descended from a decedent but instead related collaterally, such as a cousin, niece, or nephew. heir,
An attempt to defeat a judgment through a separate action instead of an appeal or other direct attack.
A doctrine holding that a judgment on issues litigated by two parties is binding on them for those issues in all subsequent actions with different causes of action.
A rule stating that if an injured person receives compensation from a source such as insurance, the damages the injured person can receive from the tortfeasor are not reduced by that amount.
Negotiation and dispute resolution over employment matters between a representative group of employees and the employer. See also union.
In an action for slander or libel, a declaration that words spoken or written by the defendant were about the plaintiff and intended by the defendant.

(1) Conspire; to agree secretly to commit some fraudulent act. (2) In a divorce proceeding, for the husband and wife to agree that one of them will commit some act that can be grounds for divorce. N. collusion.

A lawsuit brought by people who do not actually have a grievance in order to see how the court will rule or to establish a precedent; courts will not hear collusive actions because they have no real controversy.
Appearance as opposed to reality; disguise; hiding facts behind a false legal theory.
The appearance of legal authority without the substance; something that appears to have the authority of law but in fact does not.
An instrument that appears to be a valid title but in fact is not.
Presenting a deceptive appearance.
(1) Courtesy toward others. (2) The judicial practice of one court recognizing the decisions of courts from other jurisdictions out of respect. See also full faith and credit.
Recognition by one nation within its own territory of the laws of another nation.
To begin. N. commencement.
An opinion or reaction to something; an observation. V. comment.
The exchange, buying, and selling of goods, services, and property;
Article I, Section 8, Clause 3 of the U.S. Constitution, which gives Congress the power to regulate commerce with foreign nations, among the states, and with American Indian tribes.
The most common kind of bank, which provides a full range of banking services. See also savings and loan association.
A document in which the maker promises to pay the bearer a certain sum on deposit or at a particular time; a short-term unsecured loan issued by a company.
Speech that has a specific commercial purpose, such as advertising.
An item or collection of goods that cannot be divided without impairing its value, such as a machine, a suite of furniture, or a bale of cotton.
To mix; to blend together.
A fiduciary's mingling of a client's or beneficiary's money with his or her own.

(1) A fee paid to an agent, broker, etc., for handling a transaction, usually a percentage of the total transaction. (2) A warrant issued by the government or a court authorizing its recipient to perform specific duties. (3) A group of people authorized to perform some function. (4) The act of committing a crime.
A member of a commission, or a person with a commission.
(1) To do or perform a deed or act. (2) To entrust to the care of someone else; to send someone to prison or a mental institution. (3) To bind oneself to someone or something.
(1) The act of committing. (2) An obligation.
(1) A group of people appointed to perform a specific duty. (2) Someone who has been committed to a mental institution. (3) Someone appointed to care for an incompetent person.
(1) Something that can be sold; a thing of value. (2) Raw materials or agricultural products that can be bought and sold.
Property shared by two or more owners, such as tenants in common or husband and wife; property owned by all citizens and held in trust by the government. property,
A union between a man and a woman who have not been married by ceremony but who have agreed to live together as husband and wife and have assumed marital duties.
The rights that constitute the basis of marriage and that a husband and wife are entitled to expect from one another.
Shares of ownership of a company. See also preferred stock.
Parks and other land set aside for public use. Commons,
A political unit governed for the common good; a commonwealth can take many forms, such as a nation, state, republic, or union.
To exchange information. N. communication.

A law passed as part of the Telecommunications Act of 1996 that attempted to regulate obscenity on the Internet and was effectively ruled unconstitutional by the U.S. Supreme Court in the late 1990s and early 2000s. ABBRV. CDA.

(1) A locality or the people who live in it. (2) A group of people who share a religion, profession, or other characteristic. (3) A group of states or nations with common interests. (4) The sense of fellowship that comes from sharing a home, interests, or characteristics.

Property owned equally by husband and wife, with all earnings divided equally between spouses; the form of marital property distribution used by a minority of states. See also equitable distribution, marital property.
To replace or change; to replace a criminal sentence with a lighter one. N. commutation.
An agreement between two or more parties, especially states or governments, to work together on shared concerns.

Article I, Section 10, Clause 3 of the U.S. Constitution, prohibiting states to enter into compacts with other states or with foreign nations (i.e., interstate compacts) without the consent of Congress.
A group or association of people with the purpose of running a business or commercial enterprise; a business. company,

A system under which negligence is computed in terms of percentage, and a party's damages will be reduced by the amount that his or her negligence contributed to them; contributory negligence has largely replaced the doctrine of comparative negligence.
(1) To give someone money to make up for an injury he or she has suffered. (2) To pay someone for work he or she has performed. N. compensation.
redress.
Ability to stand trial or serve as a witness.
Able; capable; having the ability to do something; capable of understanding.
A court with proper jurisdiction.
Evidence that is admissible because it is material and relevant.
A party who brings a legal complaint against another.

(1) The pleading that begins a civil lawsuit, in which the plaintiff sets forth his or her causes of action and demands relief. (2) In criminal law, a charge made before a magistrate that a particular person has committed an offense, in an effort to begin the process of prosecution.
The practice within a business of ensuring that all personnel are following applicable laws, rules, and regulations.
To follow orders or rules; to submit.
(Latin) Of sound mind; sane and mentally competent. See also non compos mentis.
Interest paid on top of interest already accrued, in addition to interest paid on principal. interest,
For an injured party to agree not to prosecute the person who has committed the crime against him or her in exchange for some payment.
Interest paid both on principal and on any interest already accumulated.
An international treaty effective in 1997 that bans all nuclear explosions. ABBRV. CTBT.
Force or duress;

The mandatory addition of a party to a lawsuit if his or her presence is necessary to provide complete relief to the current parties, or if his or her interests will be substantially affected by the lawsuit. joinder,
Arbitration in which the parties are forced to agree.
Motor vehicle insurance that drivers are required by law to purchase.
A court's use of subpoena, arrest, or attachment to force a person to appear in court; a defendant's constitutional right to force witnesses to appear and testify at trial.
A federal law passed in 1986 that made hacking into computer systems illegal. ABBRV. CFAA.
Slang for a swindler. See also confidence game, flim-flam.
To hide.
The practice of carrying a concealed weapon;
A license that allows a private citizen to carry a concealed weapon.
Intentionally withholding information.
(1) The beginning of pregnancy, when an egg is fertilized by a sperm; the action of conceiving a child. (2) A notion or idea; the beginning of an idea. V. conceive.
An act that has been planned and performed by two or more people with a common purpose. See also conspiracy, accomplice.
Amicable resolution of a dispute by the parties themselves, often done before trial. See also arbitration, mediation.
(1) To end; to sum up. (2) To reach a judgment through reasoning.
(1) An ending. (2) A final opinion arrived at through examination of facts and logical reasoning.
A conclusion reached by examining only the facts and evidence, without applying any law.
A court's legal conclusions about a case, arrived at by applying rules of law.
A presumption that is so strong it cannot be rebutted by contradictory evidence, which makes it effectively not a presumption, but a rule of law. presumption,
Incontrovertible evidence; evidence that establishes a proposition and that cannot be disproved.
To agree.

A concurring opinion; an opinion written by a judge or justice of an appellate court agreeing with the majority conclusion in a case but describing a different path of legal reasoning. See also dissent.
A sentence served simultaneously with another sentence. sentence,
An opinion written by a judge who is part of a panel of judges, agreeing with the conclusion of the majority opinion but not necessarily with the reasoning. opinion,

(1) To find someone guilty of a crime; to sentence someone to death. (2) To disapprove of publicly. (3) To officially declare a building unfit for use. (4) To use eminent domain to take private property for public use. N. condemnation.
Something that must happen or be the case before something else can happen; a prerequisite. ADJ. conditional.
A fact or state of affairs that must exist before a particular contractual duty must be performed.
A future event that will end a contractual obligation.
A pardon that becomes or remains valid as long as the pardoned person does or does not observe some specified condition. pardon,
A kind of dwelling arrangement in which individuals own separate units in a multi-unit complex and all owners share common facilities and areas. See also cooperative.
In marriage, the forgiveness of some behavior that would be grounds for divorce by resuming cohabitation on the condition that the behavior not happen again.
To approve, albeit reluctantly; to allow undesirable behavior to continue.
An alliance or league of states, each of which retains full sovereignty and self-government, with a limited central government.
To admit to having committed a crime or wrongful act. N. confession.
A pleading in which the party admits the truth of the allegations in the complaint but then presents new information that makes those allegations ineffectual. See also affirmative defense.
Entry of judgment against a debtor without a legal proceeding, done if the debtor signs a cognovit note at the time of purchase.
A swindle in which a swindler earns the trust of the victim and then takes advantage of it to cheat the victim out of money; informally called a con game or simply con.
Statements made in private, meant to be heard only by the person addressed. See also privilege.
(1) To show that a statement is correct; to state that a fact is true. (2) To formally install someone in office; to ratify.
Formal approval; written ratification of something.
To take someone's private property for public use; during wartime, to take an enemy's property; for the government to appropriate private property without compensation. N. confiscation.
An ethical dilemma in which a person is entrusted with two duties at odds with one another, in which attention to one duty will harm the other.

The conflict that results when a controversy occurs in more than one jurisdiction, thus raising the question of which jurisdiction's law to apply to the matter; the branch of law that considers these questions, also called choice of law.
To comply with stated rules or standards. ADJ.
An exact copy of a document with handwritten notations of items that were written by hand on the original; often prepared and certified by a clerk of court.
conformity. See also nonconformity.
To face someone; to come face to face with a problem or an accusation. N. confrontation.

A provision of the Sixth Amendment of the U.S. Constitution guaranteeing the accused the right to confront his or her accuser and witnesses at trial, to hear his or her testimony, and cross-examine him or her.
To make blurry or perplexing; to intermingle things so that they cannot be distinguished from one another. N. confusion.
Mixing the property of different owners so that they can no longer identify their own goods.
A corporate entity composed of several different corporations merged together. See also merger.

(1) A country's national legislative body; in the United States, the body composed of the Senate and the House of Representatives, usually capitalized to "Congress." (2) A formal meeting, usually by political or professional groups. (3) A union or coming together, as in the phrase "sexual congress."
usage_examples: (2) A formal meeting, usually by political or professional groups. (3) A union or coming together, as in the phrase

definition: A tentative conclusion or opinion based on limited information; a guess. V. conjecture.
Having to do with the marital relationship.
Rights that each spouse can expect from the other, particularly sexual rights but also the rights to society, comfort, and affection.
A visit to a convict by his or her spouse during which the couple is given the opportunity to have sexual intercourse.
A denial of all allegations as entirely false. denial,
A single collective denial of several facts stated in a complaint.
Blood relationship; the relationship of people who share a common ancestor. ADJ. consanguine, consanguineous. consanguinity,
A personal standard that guides a person's view of what is right and wrong; moral sense.
An equity court's application of community standards of fairness and decency along with principles of equitable law and precedent to its resolution of a controversy.
A person who refuses to serve in the armed forces due to ethical, moral, or religious objections to war. See also pacifist.
One who has been compulsorily enrolled in the military. V. conscript.
The draft; compulsory enlistment in the military.
To agree voluntarily; to give permission. N. consent. consent,
A binding decree issued by a judge that expresses a consensual agreement between the parties to a lawsuit.
A judgment that the parties agree to.
A search made by police after the subject of the search has voluntarily consented to it freely, which absolves the police of the need for a search warrant.
A result; the result that naturally follows a cause.

Damages for an injury that arises from the consequences of an act, for loss that arises from an injury but is not directly caused by the injury and would not necessarily occur; also called special damages. damages,
A guardian; someone appointed by a court to manage an estate, a business, or the personal affairs of someone unable to manage alone.
The payment or reward essential to the formation of a contract and that persuades a person to enter the contract; something of value given in exchange for a performance or a promise.
A dictum that is so well-developed it has gained an air of authority as if it were part of an opinion.
To entrust goods to someone else; to deliver goods to the custody of a carrier or agent, usually so that they can be sold.
The recipient of consigned goods; the person named as recipient in a bill of lading.
(1) The act of consigning. (2) A batch of goods consigned to someone.
To put together several things into one coherent whole. N. consolidation.
A single appeal filed by more than one appellant whose interests are similar enough to make their combination feasible.

term: Consolidated Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act of 1985
definition: A federal law that allows some employees to maintain health coverage through an employer after leaving employment. ABBRV. COBRA.
Combining several related lawsuits into a single action.
Combining two or more corporations by destroying them and creating a new corporation with the assets and liabilities of the now nonexistent ones. See also merger.

(1) A married person's right to fellowship, help, and affection from his or her spouse. (2) An association of several companies that join together to pursue a common object for a specified period of time, in which the members do not assume liability for one another's actions. PL. consortia. consortium,
Two or more people who join together to plan and commit an unlawful act. V. conspire.
A person who participates in a conspiracy.
One who authorizes someone else to act for him or her, usually used to name the members of a community that elect and are represented by a legislator or politician.
A collection of fundamental principles of law according to which a nation or organization is to be governed.
The authority to act in particular matters granted to branches of the government by the Constitution; see also implied powers, enumerated powers, Commerce Clause, necessary and proper clause. power,
A legal question that must be answered by interpreting the Constitution, as opposed to being addressed by a statute. See also unconstitutional.
An idea composed of various conceptual elements, usually one that is not empirically verifiable or testable.
(1) The interpretation of a statute or legal authority, particularly of ambiguous portions of it. See also construe. construction,
A person who interprets a law, especially the Constitution, in a particular way. (2) Building houses and other structures.
Any form of service other than personal service. service,
A contract not intentionally entered into by the parties that arises legally to prevent injustice. Synonymous with quasi contract.
A situation in which an employee finds conditions at work so intolerable that he or she feels forced to resign, without actually being discharged by the employer. See also harass, discrimination.
A situation in which a landlord lets living conditions get so bad that a tenant feels compelled to move out.
Notice implied by law, usually because it is in a public record.
The power to control an item without actual physical possession of it.
To find the meaning in words or actions. See also construction.
An expert, often an independent contractor, paid by a company to give it advice on some matter but not employed by that company.
A person who buys goods and services and consumes or uses them.
Goods bought for personal use by an individual, household, or family, and not intended for resale; a category of goods under the Uniform Commercial Code.

Federal and state laws regulating sales and credit practices, intended to protect consumers from deceptive advertising, poor quality, dishonest credit and debt practices, and other dangers facing retail consumers who cannot engage in arm's-length bargaining with retailers and manufacturers.

A health plan that is meant to give consumers a higher degree of control over their own health care, typically by allowing consumers to save money and use it to pay for medical care as they choose. See also health savings account, high deductible health plan.
Disregard for authority; willful disobedience or disrespect.
Disobedience to a court and its officers; any act intended to interfere with the operation or dignity of the court.
Touching, next to, sharing a border.
A congress held by the American colonies at the start of the American Revolution; Continental Congresses were held in 1774, 1775, and 1776, and resulted in the creation of the Continental Army.

A fee charged by an attorney that is contingent on a successful outcome of the case; often the attorney will take a percentage of the client's recovery if the client prevails, and will receive nothing if the client loses; commonly used by attorneys representing plaintiffs in personal injury lawsuits. Also called a contingent fee.
A bequest that will take effect when and if a specified event occurs.
Someone who will receive the proceeds of a trust or some other arrangement only if a specific event occurs.
An estate that will only vest if a specific event occurs.
An interest that will only vest if a specific event occurs.
Potential liability; liability that will accrue if some specific event occurs first.
A right that will only vest if a specific event occurs.
Postponement or adjournment of a trial or other court proceeding to a later date.
A permanent state of trespass, as when someone builds a house on his or her own land that encroaches onto the property of someone else. trespass,
(Latin) Against.
Illegal goods; goods that are illegal to possess, such as narcotics or counterfeit money.

FOB. free trade agreement. An agreement between two or more nations, states, or other geographic units that requires each member to allow other members to sell specified goods freely within its territory, without imposing tariffs or duties. See also North American Free Trade Association, NAFTA.
A person who contracts to do work for someone else, on an independent basis, using his or her own materials and methods and not under the control of the customer with regard to
To give; to donate something in order to help some cause; to help or assist in bringing something about.

A payment of a portion of a liability recovery by one who shares liability, especially among insurers; the doctrine that allows someone who has already paid a judgment to recover part of the amount from another party who is also liable. ADJ. contributory.
A plaintiff's negligence that is at least a partial proximate cause of the injury. negligence,
Power over someone or something; power to direct a person's actions, manage a machine, or influence events. V. control.
A drug regulated by law whose distribution is restricted or prohibited because of potential for abuse, including narcotics, stimulants, depressants, hallucinogens, and marijuana.
Federal and state laws that regulate distribution and use of controlled substances.
A dispute that can be litigated in court; a civil action or lawsuit.
Willful disobedience of the court's authority.
Contemptuous insolence; rudeness or insulting language.
(1) An agreement, usually among several nations, that is less formal than a treaty. (2) A meeting of members of a professional, political, or social organization.
The tort of wrongfully taking another person's property and assuming ownership of it or preventing the owner from using it. V. convert.
Bonds or preferred stock that can be exchanged for common stock or another lesser security, generally within the same company.
To transfer property from one person to another. N. conveyance.
For a court to find someone guilty of a crime. N. One who has been found by a court to be guilty of a crime.
The legal act of finding someone guilty of a crime; the end of the prosecution, including the judgment or sentence. conviction,
A business owned and run by its members for the purpose of mutual help, in which profits and costs are shared.
An apartment in a complex of apartments in which each owner has a share in the entire complex and a lease of his or her own apartment. See also condominium.
A set fee a person pays for a medical service received as part of a health insurance plan.

The legal right to publish, perform, or display a work of literature, art, music, drama, recording, or film, protected both by common law and by statute, including the Copyright Act of 1976. See also infringe.
A public official who investigates sudden, violent, and suspicious deaths. See also inquest.
Physical punishment, such as beating.
A corporation's power to act in particular matters that are necessary to its operation.

The opportunity available to someone closely related to a corporation to use corporate information or appropriate corporate business opportunities for his or her own personal gain; the corporate opportunity doctrine prohibits this kind of opportunism.

The legal entity created by law of an association of people who hold shares of a business, existing as an artificial person that can sue and be sued and whose liability is generally limited to corporate assets. corporation, C. N. A corporation formed under Subchapter C of the Internal Revenue Code; also called a Subchapter C corporation. corporation,
Having a physical body.
(Latin) Body; a collection or mass; the principal mass of a physical substance. In trust law, it is the principal or res of an estate.

(Latin) The body of a crime; the person or object on which the crime is committed, or the prima facie showing that the person or object was the victim of a crime; a necessary element for conviction, including the occurrence of some injury and some criminal act.
(Latin) Body of law; a book that collects principles of law.
(Latin) A collection of canon law.
(Latin) A collection of civil law.
A jail, reformatory, prison, or other institution where those convicted of crimes are sent.
The government system of correctional institutions and parole systems.
To confirm or verify; to agree with or give support to. N. corroboration.
Evidence that agrees with or supports evidence that has already been presented.
Willing to act dishonestly in exchange for money or advantage;
Abuse of an official position for personal gain; dishonest behavior by someone who holds official power. See also bribe.
To sign a document along with someone else, either to guarantee the first signer's obligation or to verify the authenticity of the other signature.
One who cosigns a document. See also surety.

The price; the amount that must be paid for something. cost, insurance, and freight. A term meaning that the cost of goods, insurance, and shipping are included in the contract price. ABBRV. C.I.F., C.F.I. cost,
The amount a taxpayer paid for an asset.

In a breach of contract action, a measure of damages calculated by the amount it would cost to pay for a performance that would put the plaintiff where he or she would have been had the contract not been breached.
The amount it costs to live from day to day, measured by the Consumer Price Index.
A kind of contract, common in construction projects, in which the contractor is paid for costs of material and labor plus a percentage of those costs as profit.
The expenses involved in taking a case to trial, sometimes awarded to the victorious party; costs usually do not include attorney's fees.
A tenancy shared by two or more people at the same time, including joint tenancy and tenancy in common.
Someone who shares a tenancy with someone else.
(1) An attorney, a lawyer; also called counselor. (2) Legal advice. V. To give legal advice.
A distinct statement of a cause of action or a claim in a plaintiff's pleading; an allegation of a specific offense or charge in an indictment.
A claim made by a defendant to oppose the claim brought by the plaintiff; a counterdemand. counterclaim,
Fake; forged; copied with the intention of passing off as authentic. V. counterfeit.
A person who makes fake copies of money or other objects and passes them off as genuine.

term: counting individuals and dividing something among them equally
definition: per capita.
A locally governed territorial division of a state; called a parish in Louisiana.
A certificate attached to a loan instrument that can be separated from the instrument and presented after a specified time for the collection of interest.
A bond that has coupons attached to it for the collection of several installments of interest before the bond matures.
Normal business operations.

A series of previous acts and conduct between the parties to a transaction that establishes an understanding that subsequent actions and conduct related to the same or similar transactions will be interpreted the same way. See also trade usage.
The normal duties and activities associated with a job; the activities that an employee performs as his or her customary services to the employer.

A tribunal in which a judge and/or jury hears and decides civil and/or criminal cases; the governmental entity that applies the state's laws to cases and controversies and administers justice; also called a court of law. court,
A court that hears cases and makes judgments according to principles of equity; see also chancery.
A court that hears appeals of decisions made by trial courts. See also appellate court.
A court that hears cases in equity, particularly cases that would not have a suitable remedy at law; most states no longer have courts of equity. See also chancery.
The highest court to which an appellant can hope to appeal, such as the Supreme Court for federal cases.
A person trained and certified to record testimony at trial or at a deposition and produce an official transcript of it.
The practice by the president of appointing to federal benches only judges who espouse a particular ideological viewpoint, in order to increase the likelihood that courts will rule in that direction.

(1) A military court that hears cases involving members of the armed forces. (2) A trial or proceeding before a military court. V. To try someone in a court-martial. PL. courtsmartial or court-martials.
A contract or formal agreement; often produced in writing and signed by all parties. V. To enter into an agreement. covenant,
A covenant in which a seller of land agrees to do whatever is necessary to perfect the buyer's title.
An agreement made as part of a contract for employment in which the covenantor agrees not to compete with the covenantee in a particular area for a specified time.
A covenant that is attached to a piece of land and is binding on those who come into possession of it.
A person for whom a covenant is made.
A person who makes a covenant.

(1) In insurance, to protect against loss or liability; to furnish money to pay for the financial consequences of an accident or misfortune. (2) In commercial law, after a seller breaches a promise to deliver goods, to find an alternative source of the same goods on the open market.
In insurance, the amount that the insurer is prepared to pay for a particular loss.
In common law, the legal state of a married woman, which was generally merged with that of her husband, leaving her without the right to own property or enter contracts on her own.
Certified public accountant.
outline.

A nonprofit organization dedicated to making creative work more freely available by providing free licensing services to creators of literary works, enabling copyright holders to grant some of the rights to their work to the public.
The quality, especially in a witness, of being believable and likely to tell the truth.
Believable; trustworthy.
Evidence that is likely to be true because it is natural and probable in light of other circumstances already known.
A trustworthy witness whose testimony is believable.

(1) The ability of a person or business to obtain money, goods, or services because of the likelihood that he or she will pay back any debt on schedule, usually based on a past history of payment and likelihood of future success. (2) Money lent on credit. (3) In accounting, a sum received; the opposite of debt.
A number calculated by examining a person's assets, liabilities, and financial history that tells a lender how likely that person is to pay back a loan.
A person or business to whom a debt is owed. See also debtor.
A proceeding brought in equity by a creditor who has secured judgment against a debtor to enforce payment of the debt if the creditor cannot reach the debtor's property at law.
An act that violates criminal law. crime,

An attack on or persecution of a group of people who are part of a widespread practice or governmental policy that results in serious attacks on human dignity or degradation of the victims, including widespread rape, murder, torture, or racial, political, or religious persecution.
Sexual intercourse that violates statutorily mandated standards of behavior. See also buggery, sodomy.
A crime that is illegal because it is wrong in itself. See also malum in se.
A crime that is illegal because it is prohibited by statute. See also malum prohibitum.
A crime committed in the heat of sudden passion.
Trespassing on property that has been posted with signs warning away trespassers, or remaining on someone else's property after being ordered to leave.
The government's system of law enforcement that apprehends, prosecutes, and punishes criminals, including law enforcement officers, courts, and correctional institutions.
The branch of law that deals with the prosecution and punishment of criminals; the body of law that determines what constitutes a crime and what constitutes suitable punishment.
Helping a party to a lawsuit prosecute or defend the case in such a way as to obstruct justice and promote needless litigation.
The crime of maliciously injuring property.
The body of law that governs actions in criminal courts, including investigation, prosecution, and punishment.
An official history of a person's arrests, convictions, sentences, and paroles; also called a rap sheet.
To decide that some act is a crime; to pass a law stating that an act is a crime; see also decriminalize.
scrutinize.
A claim brought by a party to a lawsuit against a coparty, out of an occurrence that is part of the subject matter of the original action. See also counterclaim.

At trial or a deposition, the examination of a witness by the party opposed to the side that produced him or her, regarding testimony already raised on direct examination and matters of witness credibility.
As a grounds for divorce, treatment by one spouse that causes the other spouse suffering and makes life together completely unbearable.
Punishment that offends ordinary people due to its excessiveness or cruelty, or that is far out of proportion to the offense it punishes; forbidden by the Eighth Amendment of the Constitution.
Comprehensive Test Ban Treaty.
(Latin) Fault, blame.
A person who commits a crime or wrongful act; a person accused of a crime.
Multiple sentences that are served one after another; also called consecutive sentences.

A system of shareholder voting in which a shareholder is allowed to apply all of his or her votes to one candidate for a board of directors instead of having to cast separate votes for each office, allowing minority shareholders a chance for meaningful representation.
Able or intended to correct a legal error.
An instruction given by a judge to a jury intended to correct an erroneous instruction or to fix the jury's interpretation of improper evidence.
A statute passed to correct a defect in a previously enacted law. See also declaratory.
Coined money and banknotes in general circulation.
Current return on an investment measured as a percentage of its current price.
Property of a business that could or will be converted into cash as part of normal operations in the immediate future, usually within the year.
Liabilities that are part of normal business operations and that will be repaid within a year.

A common-law right of a husband to take a life estate in all of his wife's lands if she dies before him, usually only applicable if they had children together; curtesy has been abolished or modified in most states. See also dower.
The land surrounding and attached to a house, often enclosed in some way.
A sentence that involves imprisonment. sentence, death. See death penalty. sentence,
One who cares for something, ranging from buildings to financial assets.
(1) Responsibility or guardianship of a person or thing. (2) Imprisonment. custody,
The daily nurture and control of a child, usually awarded to one parent in a case of divorce or separation.
The traditional, ordinary, and accepted way of doing things in a particular community.
A customary practice that has been done so long that it has acquired the force of law in its locality.
Ordinary; according to usual practices or custom; habitual.
Someone who buys goods or services from a business.

(1) The government agency that collects tariffs and taxes on imports; also the place at an airport, port, or border crossing where imported goods are inspected. (2) Tariffs or taxes on imported goods; also called customs duties.
A crime committed using computers, networks, or the Internet.
The field of law that encompasses issues involving computers, networks, and information technology.
The nonphysical territory occupied by the Internet and computer networks.

Registering a trademarked name as a domain name with the intention of selling it to an individual or company that actually owns the trademarked name. cy-près. ADJ. (French) As near; as near as possible; the doctrine that if a bequest is illegal or impossible, the court will try to replace it with one as close to the testator's intentions as possible. ADV. cy-près.
Doing business as.
A daily allowance of money.
Physical harm;
Money awarded as compensation for injury or loss. damages,
(Latin) Harm without injury; a loss or injury that does not give rise to a legal action to recover damages.
(Latin) Harm due to fate; an injury caused by an act of God or some force beyond human control. See also act of God.
Able to harm or injure; risky or hazardous.
An object that can endanger people by either careless or improper use.
A device that can inflict serious injury or death; see also deadly weapon.
The day on which a litigant is appointed to appear in court and be given the opportunity to seek relief from an injury or to defend himor herself from charges.

(Latin) Conditionally; in preparation for a possible future need; usually used to describe a legal proceeding that is done provisionally and let stand for the time being, but that can be challenged in the future. de bene esse,
(Latin) In fact, in reality; used to describe a situation that is for all practical purposes the case, though it might not be legal or official; see also de jure.
Authority that exists in reality.
A government that sets itself up in place of the legal government, often by use of force.
Someone who performs the duties of a judge but who does not have legitimate judicial authority.
A union of two parties who live together as husband and wife but in which the legality of the marriage is in some way defective.
(Latin) By law, by right; the condition of being in compliance with all applicable laws; legitimate and lawful. See also de facto.
(Latin) Insignificant or unimportant; not of a level to interest the law.
A crime that is not important enough to justify judicial involvement. de minimis non curat lex. (Latin) The law does not care about insignificant matters.
(Latin) Anew; starting again at the beginning; doing something a second time as if the first time had not occurred. ADV. de novo.
A second trial for a matter that has already been adjudicated; starting over as if the first trial had never happened.
The continuing control by the dead over the affairs of the living; see also mortmain.
Force likely to cause death or serious bodily injury. force,
Force that could kill the person against whom it is used.
A weapon or instrument that is capable of killing someone, whether because of its inherent nature (such as a gun) or the way it is used (such as a hammer). See also weapon.

To conduct business; to buy and sell a particular commodity. N. (1) An arrangement between two or more parties that works to their mutual benefit. (2) A transaction that involves buying or selling something.
A person who buys and sells a particular commodity; one who buys something in order to resell it. See also retail, broker.
A warrant issued by a governor or other high officer ordering a law enforcement officer to execute a convicted criminal who has been sentenced to death. warrant,
An amount paid to a beneficiary when an insured person dies.
An official document issued to certify that someone is dead, usually including cause and time of death.
Capital punishment; punishing someone for a serious crime such as murder by killing him or her.
Official records of deaths kept by a municipal authority.
Any tax imposed on property or its transfer after its owner dies.
To corrupt; to destroy one's morals or manners; to seduce.
Overindulgence in pleasures of the senses; sexual immorality.
An unsecured loan instrument issued by a company and backed by a promise to pay or general credit rather than a
In accounting, a sum of money that is charged and due; the opposite of credit.
Something, usually money, that is owed; an obligation to pay a certain amount; see also bad debt. debt,
The practice of combining debts from various sources into one account.
(1) A person who owes a debt to someone else; see also creditor. (2) A person subject to bankruptcy proceedings. debtor,
Someone who has died; a dead person. ADJ. Dead.
Someone who has died.
A fraudulent misrepresentation used to cheat someone; the act of deceiving someone by lying or misrepresenting the truth. ADJ. deceitful.
To make someone believe something that is not true; to mislead.
To come to a conclusion or resolution after deliberation and consideration; to determine something.
A conclusion reached after considering facts and applicable law if necessary; a judicial determination or judgment. See also opinion.

(1) A formal announcement or statement. (2) At common law, the first pleading presented to the court by a plaintiff, similar to a complaint in structure and purpose. (3) In evidence, an unsworn statement made by a witness, a party to a lawsuit, or a person who subsequently died. (4) When entering a country, a statement of goods brought into the country by the person entering. declaration,
A statement made out of court that is so disadvantageous to the person making it that it is presumed to be true; declarations against interest are admissible as an exception to the hearsay rule.

A document written by Thomas Jefferson and adopted by the thirteen colonies on July 4, 1776, listing the colonies' grievances against England and declaring the colonies a separate nation free from allegiance to Great Britain.
A formal public announcement by the leadership of a nation that it is at war with another nation.
Explanatory; meant to clarify an ambiguity.
A judgment issued by a court in which it determines the legal rights of the parties or expresses an opinion
A statute enacted to clarify the law on a particular matter, intended to end any doubts as to its meaning. See also curative.

(1) To announce something formally, such as war; to make a pronouncement about the state of something, such as naming something a fire hazard. (2) In taxation, to admit to having received income; when going through customs upon entering a country, to admit to possessing goods on which duty is owed.

The judicial decision of a court in equity made after hearing testimony and determining the rights of the parties, equivalent to a judgment by a court of law, though the term "judgment" may also be used in reference to courts of equity.
A conditional judgment that will be made permanent unless a party can show cause why it should not be; common in actions for divorce. See also consent decree.
To legalize an act that was formerly criminal.
For a private owner to convey land or an easement to the public for public use. N. dedication.
To subtract something from a total; in taxation, to subtract the cost of items from total income to determine taxable income. N. deduction.
Able to be deducted; items that may be deducted from gross income are described as tax-deductible. N. The amount of money that an insured party must pay for a loss before an insurer will pay a claim.
A written instrument, signed and delivered, by which one person conveys land to another.
A deed that releases property from an encumbrance, such as a mortgage.
A deed that transfers the title of property to a trustee as a security; used instead of a mortgage in some states.
A deed made by and binding a single party.
In corporate bankruptcy, a principle that prevents controlling shareholders from presenting their claims before or with those of other creditors.
To embezzle funds that have been entrusted to the embezzler; to fail to pay over trust funds or other money held in a fiduciary capacity at the proper time. N. defalcation.
An intentional publication or public statement of false information that damages someone's reputation. V.
defamatory. See also libel, slander.
Failure to perform a legal duty or meet an obligation.
Judgment entered against a defendant who fails to defend himself or herself by responding to the plaintiff's complaint or appearing in court.
The action of rendering something null and void; an instrument that negates or nullifies some other instrument, such as a will or deed.
Able to be revoked, nullified, or undone; usually applied to estates and land interests that are subject to a conditional limitation and are not absolute.
An imperfection or a flaw; a lack or deficiency. defect,
Flawed or insufficient; lacking something necessary to completion.
A pleading that is insufficient or inaccurate in substance or form.
Unmarketable title; a title obtained by fraud or not in fact entirely owned by the purported owner.
To resist an attack; to shield or repel; to represent a defendant in a lawsuit. See also defendant, defense.
The party against whom a lawsuit is brought; in civil cases, the party who responds to the complaint; in criminal cases, the person against whom charges are brought.

A response, reason, or allegation offered by a defendant to a lawsuit as to why the plaintiff has not established a claim and should not receive relief; a denial of the plaintiff's claims or an attack on the validity of the plaintiff's causes of action. See also affirmative defense.
An attorney who normally represents defendants in lawsuits.
A law firm that specializes in representing defendants in lawsuits, often paid by the hour. See also insurance defense, plaintiff's firm.
To put off to a later time; to postpone. N. deferment, deferral.
Postponing the payment of taxes to a later year.
A sentence that is postponed to some future time and possibly never imposed if the defendant does not violate the terms of probation. sentence,

Compensation that is taxed when it is received as opposed to when it is earned, such as contributions made by an employer to an employee's retirement plan that are not taxed until the retirement funds are distributed.
Payments that are spread out over time or entirely postponed.
A lack; a shortcoming; in taxation, the amount of tax owed that has not been paid on time.
A judgment issued against someone who holds a mortgage, imposing personal liability on him or her if a foreclosure sale does not yield enough money to cover the mortgage debt.
A shortfall; an insufficiency; the condition brought about by spending more money than is earned; in accounting, the opposite of surplus.
A sentence set by law for a particular crime, not modifiable by the judge; also called determinate sentence or fixed sentence. sentence,
fornicate.
To take money or property from someone through the use of fraud or deceit; to cheat someone; to misrepresent a fact intending for someone to rely on it and thereby harm him or her. See also fraud.
(1) The amount, level, or extent of something. (2) A certificate awarded to those who finish a course of study at a university or college.
A classification of crime based on the level of guilt involved in committing it.
A measurement of different kinds of negligence, used to determine whether someone is sufficiently negligent to be liable for an injury.
The level of proof needed to convince a court to render a verdict for a particular case; see also preponderance of the evidence, clear and convincing, reasonable doubt.
A duty that can be performed by someone other than the one originally intended to do it.

To entrust someone with a task or responsibility; to transfer one's authority to another person. N. A person appointed to act for someone else; a person elected or appointed to represent others at a conference or assembly. ADJ. delegable.
(1) A group of representatives or delegates. (2) The transfer of power by one branch of the government to another.
To consider; to ponder; to examine reasons for and against something in order to come to a decision. ADJ. Slow and careful; done with full awareness and intention.

Immediately; as quickly as practical considerations of law, order, and the welfare of the people will allow; often used to describe the speed expected by those enacting statemandated school desegregation.
Careful consideration of a matter, weighing all arguments and evidence.
A violation of the law; a tort, injury, or crime. (Latin) delictum. delicto,
Neglect of duty; the commission of minor crimes.
(1) Of a person, neglecting one's duty; failing to pay a debt; guilty of a crime or failure of duty. (2) Of a debt, due and unpaid.
A minor below a specified age who tends to commit crimes or otherwise engages in immoral or disobedient behavior and therefore needs treatment or supervision. See also juvenile delinquent.
To remove something from a list; usually refers to removing a security from an exchange for failing to meet the minimum requirements for listing. N. delisting.
To hand something over to someone else; to voluntarily transfer title or possession of something to someone else's possession or control. N. delivery.
To deceive someone or persuade him or her to believe something that is false.

An erroneous belief that is contrary to fact, inappropriate to the age, class, and education of the believer, and that cannot be shaken by evidence to the contrary, usually a result of some mental disorder; see also insanity. ADJ. deluded.
A note payable on demand. note,

(French) Domain; something owned. N. Ownership of land held in one's own right, not subject to the claims of a superior or occupied by tenants; the phrase "held in demesne" means occupied by the owner as opposed to tenants. ADJ. In pleading, own or original, i.e., "son assault demesne" means "his own assault."
usage_examples: means occupied by the owner as opposed to tenants. ADJ. In pleading, own or original, i.e.,

definition: An area where no military activity is permitted. ABBRV. DMZ.
To lease property; to convey property for life or for years; to bequeath. N. A lease or conveyance of property for a period of years or for life.
To show; to prove the truth of something by using logic and evidence; to use practical illustrations to show how something works or happens. N. demonstration.
A bequest of money that is to be paid out of a specific fund. legacy,
Evidence composed of real objects that appeal directly to the senses, such as maps, charts, illustrations, or the weapon used in committing a crime.
To object to a point of law or fact alleged by the opposing party on the grounds that it does not advance the interests of the party making the statement; to present a demurrer.

(French) To stop; in common law, a formal allegation by a defendant that the facts stated in the complaint are true but nevertheless are not legally sufficient to allow the case to proceed, and requesting judgment for the defendant and dismissal of the cause of action; the use of demurrer has mostly disappeared under the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. denial,
In a pleading, for the defendant to contradict the allegations made in the complaint, to state that the plaintiff's allegations are not in fact correct. N. denial.
A department of the U.S. Cabinet that is charged with protecting U.S. territory from natural and man-made hazards, including terrorist attacks. ABBRV. DHS.
A federal department that dedicates itself to the U.S. workforce, monitoring working conditions, regulating wages and retirement benefits, and gathering information.
A territory or possession governed by another country and geographically separate from the governing country; e.g., Puerto Rico is a dependency of the United States.

(1) Needing support from an outside source. (2) Contingent on some external fact or condition. N. Someone who is supported by someone else; in taxation, a dependent is usually someone related to the supporting taxpayer or someone who lives in the taxpayer's home.
Insurance protection for dependents of the insured.

In common law, something that happens in between a defendant's action and its result that occurs as a normal and predictable response to the defendant's action; usually a dependent intervening cause does not break the chain of causation and the defendant is still held liable for the result of his or her original action.
A promise in which the promisor does not have to perform until the other party has done some agreed-upon duty first.

The principle that if a person revokes a will intending to replace it with another will, the first will is actually revoked only if the second will is valid; otherwise, the first will continue to be in effect.
To use up a supply or resource; to exhaust assets or natural resources.
In taxation, a principle that allows the owner of a natural resource such as oil or gas to recover the capital cost of the property as the resource is used up.
The tax deduction allowed to owners of property containing natural resources as the resources are used up.
A witness who testifies at a deposition.
To send a foreign national from one country to another because he or she has committed a crime or is of illegal immigration status. N. deportation. See also extradite.
(1) To question a witness at a deposition. (2) To testify under oath.
A form of discovery before trial in which an attorney questions a witness under oath and a court reporter makes a transcript of the testimony, which can then be used as evidence at trial.
To decrease in value over time.
The process of spreading the cost of an asset over a period of years, deducting an allowance for wear and tear as the asset is used up or worn out. depreciation,
Attacking or plundering; robbing or pillaging.
An agent; a substitute; someone empowered by a superior to exercise the functions of the superior's office and to act on the superior's behalf. V. deputize.
An officer who has the authority to exercise the duties of the office of sheriff.
In poor condition; neglected;
Coming from, influenced by, or originating in someone or something else. N. Something that comes from another person or source. V. derive.

(1) A lawsuit brought on behalf of a corporation by a shareholder. (2) A cause of action founded on an injury to another person, such as an action for loss of consortium brought by a wife after her husband is injured.

(1) A civil action in tort based on an injury caused by criminal conduct by the defendant, in which the plaintiff seeks compensation for the injury, independent of a criminal action brought for the same offense. (2) Tort liability imposed on a principal for a wrong done by his or her agent.
The partial repeal or relaxation of a law.
Family origin; heredity; the transmission of property and goods by inheritance. descent,
The rules that determine transmission of a person's real property if he or she dies intestate, i.e., without a valid will.
A name for a product that is descriptive of what it is or does, which cannot be registered unless it has taken on a distinctive meaning over time. trademark,
To end separation of people by races at school, in the workplace, or in public facilities. N. desegregation. See also segregate.
To abandon; to leave willfully with the intention of never returning. N. desertion.
culpability.
A defect inherent in the design of a product, so that even when it is correctly made and used it poses a danger to consumers. defect,
To stop; to refrain from doing something. See also cease and desist order.
A ruler with absolute power. See also tyrant.
A form of government in which one ruler has absolute power.
A poor or needy person.
To damage something so badly it can no longer be used or repaired; to ruin something completely; to demolish or annihilate; to nullify or revoke. N.
destructive.
A condition of disuse; the state of obsolete laws and practices that are no longer applied because their subject matter no longer exists, such as laws governing carriages drawn by horses.
To hold in custody; to arrest; to keep or hold back. N. detention.

(1) Preventing a person from possessing property or goods that he or she owns rightfully. (2) A request filed by a criminal justice agency asking a correctional institution to hold a prisoner for the agency or to notify the agency when the prisoner is about to be released. detainer,
(1) Able to be known or ascertained. (2) Terminable; able to be ended if a particular condition occurs.
The decision made by a judge, court, or agency that ends a lawsuit or controversy; a final judgment.
A common law action brought to recover property that has been unlawfully taken; see also replevin.
A testamentary gift of real property made in a will. V. devise; see also bequest, bequeath.
The person who receives real property through a devise.
The person who makes the gift of real property by will.

To pass from one person to another, often as a result of the operation of law and without any intentional act by either party; often used to describe the passing of an estate from a deceased person to an heir.
Department of Homeland Security.
A statement or observation made by a judge about a case that is not an official part of a judicial opinion, does not embody the court's decision, and is not binding. PL. dicta. dictum,

A compilation or summary of a number of books, articles, cases, or other type of information, often arranged by subject and indexed for ease of reference; a listing of reported cases arranged by subject and court.
A federal law passed in 1998 that criminalizes copyright infringement of digital content as well as attempts to get around digital copyright protection measures.
The practice of restricting access to digital materials and information by copyright holders and publishers. ABBRV. DRM.
Deliberately slow; intended to delay something.
A plea by a defendant that does not address the factual merits of the complaint but instead raises procedural or
Persistence and care; attentiveness;
In corporate law, to lower the value of shares currently outstanding by issuing new shares. N. dilution.

In trademark law, a doctrine prohibiting the use of a trademark by a second user even if there is no possibility of confusion so that the uniqueness of the first user's mark will be preserved and not weakened by the presence of another use of the name.

In criminal law, a mental state that is not sufficient to have the mens rea needed to commit a crime, brought on by mental disease or defect, trauma, or intoxication; also called diminished responsibility.
Decrease; a reduction in size or number.

Power over a kind of lawsuit or person that is held by only one court or tribunal, requiring that all actions of that sort be heard there.
A right granted by the owner of a patent to one person, allowing him or her to use, make, and sell the patented item.
In trademark law, the exclusive right to use a specific mark and other marks similar enough to be confusing.
To show that someone is not guilty of a crime or wrongful act; to clear of blame or fault. ADJ. exculpatory.
A clause in a legal document that releases a party from liability for wrongdoing.
Evidence that helps clear a defendant of guilt.
The killing of a person by accident or in self-defense. homicide,
Failure to perform the right action at the right time due to unexpected and unavoidable circumstances rather than through carelessness or disregard.
To forgive; to release from responsibility. N. A reason given to justify an action. ADJ. excusable.
(1) To put into effect; to complete; to carry out an action; to sign a legal document and perform any other tasks needed to make it legally binding. (2) To put to death. N. execution.
Having the power to make things happen and carry out laws; the power held by the president of the United States.
An agreement similar to a treaty that the president of the United States can make with another country without Senate approval.
A chief executive's right to pardon criminals and commute criminal sentences.
An order issued by the president interpreting the Constitution or a law. executive powers. The power to enforce and carry out laws held by the president and governors of the states.
An executive's freedom from the disclosure laws that bind other citizens when secrecy is necessary to fulfill the duties of the office.
A person chosen by a testator to give away his or her property according to his or her will. FEMININE. executrix or executress.
Not yet complete; awaiting a future event for completion.
A contract that will not be complete until some specific event happens.
A future estate or interest in land.
A trust that has a settlement or conveyance still to make in the future.
Evidence other than testimony taken from a defendant and used to identify him or her, including fingerprints, voiceprints, blood samples, handwriting samples, etc.
Damages awarded on top of actual damages if the wrong done to the plaintiff by the defendant was aggravated in some way.
Not under an obligation that applies to others;
(1) Freedom from a normal obligation. (2) In taxation, a deduction given to a taxpayer for himor herself and for dependents.

The state of having attempted and failed to get a remedy through administrative channels before bringing a matter to litigation, or through state court before bringing a matter before a federal court.

To display something; to present something publicly. N. A piece of physical evidence or a document displayed in court or during a deposition or hearing to be made part of the case. exigence. Urgent need; an emergency; also called exigency. ADJ. exigent.

An emergency situation in which immediate action is necessary, regardless of procedural requirements; generally applied to situations in which law enforcement officials conduct a search and seizure without a warrant.
Banishment from a country; a person who has been expelled from a country. V. exile.

An interview conducted between an employee and employer when the employee leaves his or her job with that employer, at which the parties discuss the employee's job satisfaction, performance, and other matters.
To absolve; to release from blame or obligation. N. exoneration.
Something that is expected or at least hoped for; in property, an interest or estate that is expected to occur when some specific event happens in the future. See also life expectancy.
Hoping to receive something in the future.
To force out; to eject; see also expulsion.
Cost; the amount of money spent on something. expense,
Costs incurred in performing a job; money that must be spent in the ordinary course of business or daily operations. expenses,
A statement made by an expert explaining his or her evaluation of facts from a qualified perspective. opinion,
Opinions about a subject offered by an expert in the field in order to help the jury understand specialized evidence in a case.

A witness who has training or experience in a field that gives him or her knowledge about it far beyond that of an ordinary person, whose testimony is provided to help the jury understand difficult technical information.
The end of something, such as a contract period or a period of time.
To transport goods from one country to another to sell them. N. Goods made in one country and sold in another. See also import.
The act of exporting goods.
A promise included with the terms of a sale agreement, assuring the purchaser that the goods are of a particular quality and promising to fix or replace them if they are not as promised. warranty,
To take for the government under eminent domain. N. expropriation.
Driving out; ejecting from a group, school, etc; depriving of membership; see also expel.
To erase; to destroy completely, especially information or documents.
Court-ordered destruction of criminal records after a certain period of time or if an accused is not convicted.
To cleanse; to remove objectionable material from a document.
To make larger; to prolong; to push back the original boundary or deadline.
(1) An allowance of additional time after a deadline has expired. (2) An addition that makes something bigger, such as a structure added to a building to make it larger.
To mitigate; to make a crime or misdeed seem less wrong.
Circumstances that make a crime appear less serious and make the one who committed it appear less blameworthy than would ordinarily be the case.
To end; to cancel; to void a right; to quench or put out, as a fire.
The end of a right or contract by agreement between the parties or by operation of law.
To get something from someone else through force, threats, or fear. N. extortion.
blackmail.
(1) Far more expensive than is appropriate. (2) Prone to extortion.
For a state or country to transfer a person accused of a crime to the country or state where the crime supposedly occurred. N. extradition.
Done outside of court; not legally authorized.
Outside the walls, boundaries, or limits of an institution or business; in corporations, outside the limits of corporate power. See also intramural.
Unusual, remarkable.
Circumstances and factors not usually associated with a particular time, place, or situation.
A risk not normally associated with a particular activity.
