Fraud that prevents a party from knowing about and therefore defending or pursuing all legal rights available to him or her. fraud,
Federal Aviation Administration.
To invent; to create; to make up a story or manufacture an object,
Evidence created after the fact to disguise the truth.
The value of a check, bond, note, insurance policy, or other negotiable instrument as stated on the instrument itself; see also market price.
To assist; to make something possible or easier to accomplish; to make it easier for someone to commit a crime. N. facilitation. See also aid and abet.
A building, a space, or the equipment used to provide some service or perform some function; an amenity. PL. facilities.
An exact copy of something, usually a document.
Something that is true; a thing that has happened or a situation that exists.
The person or group of people whose job is to determine the facts in a case; also called the trier of fact. See also jury.
(1) Something that influences an outcome. (2) An agent who buys and sells goods for a commission called a factorage.
Truthful; describing actual events or circumstances.
Physical and mental capabilities; the ability to do something.
To be unsuccessful at something; to be deficient; to be unable to meet obligations; to lapse (a legacy). N. failure.
A condition that occurs when a contract's consideration becomes worthless or no longer exists.
The condition of producing no children to inherit one's estate; dying childless.
Miscarriage of justice; a loss of rights or lack of reparation due to the absence of an adequate legal remedy.

Inability to prove a side of a case. failure to state a claim (upon which relief may be granted). N. A plaintiff's failure to define and support a cause of action sufficient to support a case in court, resulting in dismissal of the case; see also Federal Rules of Civil Procedure.
Impartial and just; according to stated rules and standards.
A jury composed of people who have no preconceptions about a case and who will form opinions based on a fair consideration of evidence and testimony.
A trial or hearing before a court or jury that has no preconceived notions about the matter and that will hear testimony and examine evidence before making a decision.
A defense in libel cases, used especially by the news media, that the published statements in question were meant to state facts that the writer or speaker believed were true.
An administrative hearing authorized in a case where a normal judicial proceeding would not satisfy the requirements of due process, such as a case in which no judicial

A federal law passed as Title VIII of the Civil Rights Act of 1968 that prohibits discrimination in the rental, sale, and financing of housing based on race, sex, religion, national origin, handicap, and family status.
A federal statute passed in 1938 that set a minimum wage, a maximum work week, and established rules about work by teenagers for businesses engaged in interstate commerce.

The legal use of copyrighted materials without the owner's consent or payment of royalties; whether a use is a fair use or an infringement of copyright depends on factors such as who is using the material, the amount used, and whether or not the user acknowledges the copyright owner; see also copyright, plagiarize. faith-based. Religious; based in religious beliefs.
A charitable project undertaken by a religious organization.
Untrue; incorrect; deceitful or treacherous.
The deliberate inclusion of false information in advertising, with the intention of misleading consumers in order to make them purchase the advertised product. See also advertise.
Unlawfully arresting or detaining someone without legal authority or the detainee's consent.
The tort of detaining someone intentionally and without justification.
Intentionally using fraud or misrepresentation to obtain property or money.
Intentionally misleading someone either by lying or by failing to disclose a fact when the fact should have been disclosed.
A statement made by someone who knows it is false or who makes it recklessly without honestly believing it to be true, intending it to deceive.
When a person swears that something is true and he or she knows that it is not; also called a false oath.
An untruth or lie.

A group of people related to one another by blood and marriage, often sharing a common ancestor; the unit consisting of parents and children; for insurance purposes, the people who live with and are dependent on an insured person.
Money allocated to a surviving spouse and/or children to support them while the deceased spouse's estate goes through the probate process; see also widow's allowance.

term: Family and Medical Leave Act of 1993
definition: A federal law that protects the jobs of and guarantees unpaid leave to certain workers who suffer medical problems or who must care for sick family members or new children. ABBRV. FMLA.

A doctrine, also called a family purpose doctrine, that makes the owner of an automobile liable for negligent operation of it by a member of his or her family; this doctrine has been rejected by many states.
A court with jurisdiction over matters related to families and children, including child abuse and neglect, support and custody, paternity, and juvenile delinquency.
A name invented for a particular product, such as "Exxon," that is easily registered but difficult to educate consumers about. trademark,
Frequently asked questions.
Deadly; causing death; causing something to fail completely.
An error at trial that hurts one party's case so badly as to be grounds for a new trial.
An injury that causes death to its victim.
Blame; responsibility for a bad result; a wrongful act or mistake; negligence; breach of duty.
Defective.
Federal Bureau of Investigation.
Federal Communications Commission.
Food and Drug Administration.
Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation.

Sworn loyalty; in feudal times, allegiance to a feudal lord sworn by a tenant. fear, uncertainty, and doubt. N. A marketing strategy in which the manufacturer of a product tries to make consumers worry that buying a competing product might result in bad consequences, typically by criticizing or spreading disinformation about the competing product. ABBRV. FUD.

A practice by employees of increasing or maintaining the number of workers required to perform a task or the time allotted to do a specific job in an effort to protect job security, especially against the threat of technology.
Relating to the central government of a union of states, such as the national government of the United States.
The federal agency that handles civil aviation as part of the Department of Transportation. ABBRV. FAA.
The federal agency that investigates violations of federal laws, including criminal, civil, and security matters as part of the Department of Justice. ABBRV. FBI.
A body of case law composed of decisions made by federal courts, used in a very narrow range of cases; see also Erie doctrine.
The federal agency that regulates broadcasting by television, radio, telephone, satellite,

The courts of the United States, created by the Constitution or by acts of Congress and having jurisdiction created by statute, including federal district courts, federal courts of appeal, and the Supreme Court of the United States.
An agency within the executive branch of the U.S. government that insures deposits in banks and savings associations. ABBRV. FDIC.
An agency of the U.S. Department of Homeland Security dedicated to coordinating national responses to major disasters such as hurricanes or earthquakes. ABBRV. FEMA.
A license issued by the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives that allows an individual or business to manufacture or sell firearms and ammunition. ABBRV. FFL.
The act that regulates the Social Security system by taxing employers and employees and setting benefits. ABBRV. FICA.

Original jurisdiction held by federal courts over cases in which interpretation of the Constitution, acts of Congress, or treaties of the United States are in dispute; federal question jurisdiction is granted to federal courts by Article III of the Constitution.

A network of twelve central banks established by the Federal Reserve Act in 1913 to oversee a national system of banking, including monitoring the money supply by holding cash reserves and issuing currency for circulation, overseeing the credit system, and generally watching the nation's economy.
A compilation of rules created by the Supreme Court in 1938 that govern the procedure used in civil cases in U.S. district courts, which have been used as a model for procedure by most state courts.
Procedural rules created by the Supreme Court in 1945 that govern proceedings in criminal cases before U.S. district courts and sometimes before U.S. magistrates.
Rules governing the admission of evidence before U.S. district courts, U.S. magistrates, and bankruptcy court, which have been used as a model for rules of evidence by many states.
A federal agency that promotes free trade and fair competition in interstate commerce by preventing price-fixing, advertising, and general unfair competition. ABBRV. FTC.

The federal system of government, including a division of power between a central government and individual states, in which states handle local affairs and the central government handles matters that affect the entire nation.
Someone who supports federalism.

A collection of essays published by Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay from October 1787 to August 1788, advocating that New York vote to ratify the Constitution that had already been adopted in Philadelphia in September 1787.
A union of states that share a central government but also govern themselves independently.
(1) Payment made to someone in exchange for services. (2) An estate of complete ownership of land; ownership of a piece of property. fee, in. ADJ. Owning a property completely.
Complete ownership of a property, with no time limit or restrictions on what can be done with it, that will pass to the owner's heirs if he or she dies intestate; also called fee simple absolute.

An interest in property that is given by a grantor to the owner that will become a fee simple if a particular condition is met, usually having to do with the estate passing to specific heirs of the owner, and if the condition is not met the estate goes back to the grantor when the owner dies.
An estate that can end if a particular event does or does not happen; also called a fee simple determinable.

A conveyance of property that establishes a specific line of succession, limiting the transfer of the property to a particular category of descendants of the recipient, and that goes back to the grantor if there are no suitable descendants; also called an estate tail.
An estate limited to male or female lines of descent.

A coworker; someone who works for the same employer. fellow servant rule. A rule that absolved an employer from liability for injury done to one employee as a result of the negligence of another; now superseded by workers' compensation.
One who has committed a felony.
A serious crime; most felonies are defined by statute, and often include those that are punishable by death or by more than one year of imprisonment. ADJ. felonious. See also misdemeanor.

A killing that occurs while committing a felony; whether the killing occurs accidentally or not, under the felony murder doctrine it could be prosecuted as first-degree murder; this doctrine varies from state to state.
Federal Emergency Management Agency.
(1) A structure made of wood, metal, or some other material that encloses or divides a piece of land. (2) Someone who receives and sells stolen property. V. To buy and sell stolen property.
The 1896 U.S. Supreme Court case that held that separate but equal facilities for different races were constitutional.
An unborn mammal; an unborn human child after the first eight weeks of gestation. ADJ. fetal.

A social, economic, and governmental system common in medieval Europe, under which nobles gave land to vassals who fought for them, and peasants farmed the land and gave much of their produce to their lords, who in return gave them protection. ADJ. feudal.
Federal Firearms License.
An arbitrary and authoritative command; a decree.
Federal Insurance Contribution Act.

Involving trust, confidence, and good faith; describes the relationship of a trustee to a beneficiary or a guardian to a ward. N. Someone entrusted with a duty to act on behalf of and in the best interest of someone else, especially the management of someone else's property; a trustee.
The condition of acting, transacting business, or handling money on behalf of someone else.
The duty of a person or institution entrusted with someone else's property to act in that person's best interest while putting aside personal interests; the highest duty imposed by law.
First-in, first-out.

An amendment added to the Constitution as part of the Bill of Rights that provides important protections from government actions, including a guarantee that no person will be required to answer for a capital crime unless first indicted by a grand jury; no one will suffer double jeopardy; no one must testify against himor herself; no one will be deprived of life, liberty, or

Words that in and of themselves cause injury or could provoke a violent response from listeners; fighting words are not protected under the First Amendment, and uttering them can constitute the tort of assault.

The documents that comprise a case, including pleadings, motions, briefs, affidavits, and other papers. V. To deposit a document with the court or other proper authority so that it becomes part of the official record.
The practice of sharing computer files over a computer network such as the Internet.
An excessively long speech intended to obstruct debate and voting on a piece of legislature,
A document that has been filed as part of a lawsuit. See also file.
A judgment that ends a legal controversy by conclusively stating whether or not the plaintiff is entitled to relief. judgment,

A decision by the court that ends a dispute between parties and settles their respective rights, preventing further litigation of the matter unless it is reversed or set aside; also called a final judgment.
A fee charged for the extension of credit and the deferral of payment of a loan.
A business that deals in money, such as a bank or trust company, a thrift institution, a currency exchange, a securities trading company, a credit card company, an insurance company, etc.
A report of the financial condition of a person or institution, including a balance sheet, an income statement, and charges.
insolvency.
A middleman who helps people find things such as jobs, business opportunities, or loans in return for payment of a finder's fee, and then lets the parties come to their own business arrangements.
A court's or jury's conclusion about a matter of law or fact.
A court's or agency's determinations about the facts of a case after hearing testimony, examining evidence, and deliberating.
A court's determination of how to apply law to the facts of a case.
Money that a person must pay as a penalty for a crime or wrongful act. V. To punish someone by making him or her pay a fine.

Details of a contract, insurance policy, or other document that are printed in small type and placed where they will not be easily noticed or read, making it difficult for someone to learn about conditions that might be unfavorable.
The unique pattern of lines and whorls on the tip of a finger,
To end or terminate someone's employment.
A sale of merchandise at reduced prices, usually done after an emergency such as a fire or flood that destroys or damages the commercial premises.
A gun; a device that uses an explosive substance to propel a projectile from a barrel.

A federal agency within the U.S. Department of Justice that handles gun licensing and the investigation of traffic and crimes involving alcohol, tobacco, firearms, ammunition, explosives, arson, and bombings. ABBRV. ATF, BATFE.
A device or program that limits access to a computer network.

An offer by a merchant to buy or sell goods made in writing and signed, assuring the other party that the offer will be held open for a specified period of time; regulated by the Uniform Commercial Code.
An amendment to the Constitution added with the Bill of Rights, guaranteeing freedom of speech, assembly, religion, press, and the right to petition the government for redress of grievances.

The first consideration of something, particularly the first time a case appears before a court and presents an entirely new question of law that has never been considered before, and thus has no precedents to guide the decision. first-in,
An unlawful killing that is willful, deliberate, and premeditated. murder,
Killing someone deliberately and intentionally, having planned it beforehand, with extreme cruelty, or while committing another felony.

A method of inventory that assumes that the goods most recently bought are the first ones to be sold, and that goods still in inventory at the end of the year are the ones bought long ago. See also first-in,
Concerning financial matters, particularly matters of government finance and the public treasury.
A twelve-month period used by a business as a tax and accounting period; the accounting year.
Of sufficient quality or ability to meet a specific purpose;
An item's condition of being suitable for the purpose for which it is intended, usually as promised by the seller. See also warranty.
(1) To establish something permanently or for the long-term. (2) To decide or settle something; to set a price. ADJ. fixed.
The property that a business uses in its daily operations and will not convert into cash, such as equipment.
The money permanently invested in a company.
Regular income that does not fluctuate over time; income from a retirement benefit or annuity. See also price fixing.
A mortgage with an interest rate that remains the same throughout the life of the mortgage.
An object that is permanently attached to a building or property and is thus regarded as part of the property. fixture,
To run away, usually to escape danger. See also flight.
For someone who has committed a crime to leave home or the jurisdiction where the crime was committed in order to avoid arrest.
An employee benefits program that allows employees to choose from a range of possible benefits to create personalized benefits packages. See also cafeteria plan.
A savings account in which an employee can save tax-free a portion of his or her earnings to meet
The act of running away or trying to escape something; fleeing. See also flee.

term: flight 196 a specific type of expense
definition: FSA. See also cafeteria plan, health savings account.
The act by someone who has committed an unlawful act of leaving the scene of the crime in an effort to avoid arrest.

A type of fraud or confidence game performed by two perpetrators, one of whom persuades the victim to give money to the other as part of a get-rich-quick scheme, whereupon both perpetrators disappear with the money.
The time between when a check is deposited and when the money it represents is deducted from the writer's account.
A lien that affects not only inventory and assets owned by the debtor at the time the agreement is made, but also property acquired afterward.
Capital kept to meet current expenses, often kept in the form of current assets.
Short-term debt held by a business or government.
A variable interest rate that changes based on the money market.
Family and Medical Leave Act of 1993.
Free on board.
Freedom of Information Act.
A federal agency that regulates the safety of food, drugs, cosmetics, and other household products as part of the Department of Health and Human Services. ABBRV. FDA.
To resist an impulse to do something; to refrain from doing something. N. forbearance.
Compulsion;
(French) Superior strength; unforeseeable and unavoidable circumstances outside the control of a party to a contract that render him or her incapable of carrying out contractual duties.
A sale made without the owner's consent, ordered and administered by the court as part of a proceeding, such as foreclosure. sale,
People who cannot be disinherited by a testator's will, such as a spouse or children.
A sale made by order of the court to enforce a judgment against the property owner.
Done by force, compulsion, or violence.
(1) Using force to hold someone in custody. (2) A statutory remedy allowing someone who owns property to take possession of it from someone who refuses to relinquish it; see also detainer.
Using force or threat of force to illegally enter property that belongs to someone else against the owner's will.
A statutory proceeding that returns property to someone who has been wrongfully deprived of it.
To end someone's right to a property; to take possession of mortgaged property as a result of the property owner's failure to make payments. N. foreclosure.
A judgment issued by a court in another state or country. judgment,
A corporation that does business in one state but is incorporated in another.
A court in another country or state.
A person who is in one country but who is a legal permanent resident of another.
(1) A juror who leads jury proceedings and speaks to the court on the entire jury's behalf. (2) A worker in a leadership position over his or her coworkers. Also called a foreperson.
Relating to law and courts.
A branch of engineering that applies engineering principles to legal matters.
A branch of medicine that applies medical principles to the investigation of legal matters, especially the investigation of crimes that result in death.
The application of scientific tests and techniques to the investigation of crimes.
To predict; to know in advance what is likely to occur in a given situation. ADJ.
foreseeability.
The risk that a person of ordinary intelligence and prudence should reasonably expect to occur.
To lose the right to something as a punishment; to be forced to give something up. N. A right or thing lost as a penalty; the act of forfeiting something. ADJ. Lost or given up as a penalty.
Able to be given up or taken as a punishment.
The taking of property without compensation, usually as a punishment for breaking the law.
To make a copy or imitation of something intending to pass it off as the genuine article; see also counterfeit.
The copying of a document, signature, work of art, or bank note with the intention of deceiving someone into thinking it is genuine.

(1) A model or frame of a legal document with blank spaces to be filled out with information that applies to an individual situation. (2) Customary procedure or formalities; the way something is expressed as opposed to what is expressed.
The form that most U.S. taxpayers must fill out to file their income tax returns.

term: forming an entity or unit by itself
definition: (1) To divide; to move apart; to place a boundary between things. (2) For a judge to order witnesses at a trial to stay out of the courtroom except for when they are testifying. (3) For a husband and wife to move into different homes intending to no longer live together as a couple, often as a precursor to divorce; if the separation is ordered by a court,

The categories of legal actions available under common law, such as trespass, debt, etc., and divided into actions at equity and actions at law; now all forms of action are gathered together under the Rules of Civil Procedure into the single heading "civil action."
Sexual relations between two unmarried people, or by an unmarried person (who commits fornication) and a married person (who commits adultery);
Right away; immediately; at the first opportunity.
A court or tribunal; a place where people can try to get a judicial or administrative remedy for some wrong.

(Latin) An inconvenient court; a court's discretionary power to refuse to hear a case that is within its jurisdiction if it would be more convenient for the parties or better serve the interests of justice for it to be heard elsewhere.
An attempt by a litigant to have the case tried in a specific jurisdiction that appears likely to rule in his or her favor.
(1) To establish something, usually an institution or charity, often by providing an endowment for it. (2) To serve as a basis for something.

(1) The lowest part of a building that supports the weight of the entire structure. (2) The establishment of an institution, hospital, charity, school, etc. (3) A fund that supports an institution, hospital, charity, school, etc. (4) Evidence and questions of a witness used to establish the relevance of other evidence, thus making it admissible.
One who establishes a charity or institution by endowing it or supplying initial funds.
The men who created the U.S. Constitution in 1787.
The four conditions that must exist in order to create a joint tenancy under common law, including unity of interest, unity of time, unity of possession, and unity of title.
A rule requiring that the meaning of a document and the intention of its creator be determined by considering it as a whole and not in isolated parts.
quarterly.
(1) The right to vote. (2) A privilege granted by the government or a company to a person or company, allowing it to
A person who owns a franchise of a business.
A business or other entity that sells the right to engage in a particular activity or run a particular business.
Brotherly; having common interests.

An organization whose members either belong to the same professional field or who are working toward the same goal that exists to promote a common cause and assist its members with benefits for death, injury, or illness; also called a fraternal benefits society.
Intentional misrepresentation of the truth done to cause someone else to rely on that misrepresentation and be deceived into surrendering a legal right or otherwise being injured. ADJ.
Fraud that arises when a person signs a document that is not the document he or she believes is being signed.
Fraud in which a person is misled about the details of a transaction or contract and enters into an agreement under a false impression about what is involved.
defraud. fraud,
Hiding a material fact that legally or morally should be disclosed.
A transfer of property done to hinder a creditor or place the property out of a creditor's reach.
A false statement about a material fact made by a speaker who knows it is false but intends the hearer to believe it and rely on it, and that injures the hearer if he or she does rely on it.
Of property, unencumbered by liens.
A clause in the First Amendment of the Constitution stating that Congress will pass no law prohibiting the free exercise of religion; see also establishment clause.
To release someone from an obligation or liability.

Describes a contractual agreement by a seller to deliver goods to a specified place without expense to the buyer, in which the seller assumes all delivery expenses and risks; such a contract is called an f.o.b.
Liberty; absence of restrictions or restraints.
The right to enter into contracts and agreements with others, guaranteed by Article I of the Constitution.

A federal law, 5 U.S.C.A. section 552, that attempts to prevent abuse of governmental power by requiring federal agencies to make documents and information about agency matters available to the general public, with a few exceptions. ABBRV. FOIA.
The right to publish views and opinions without interference by the government, guaranteed by the First Amendment of the Constitution.
The right to choose and practice a religion without interference from the government or from established churches, guaranteed by the First Amendment of the Constitution.
The right to express one's opinions verbally or in writing without interference or restriction from the government, guaranteed by the First Amendment of the Constitution.
A piece of land held in fee or as a life estate; property that the owner holds at least for life and might also be able to pass on after death; see also leasehold.
Software made available by its copyright holder for use for an unlimited time free of charge. See also public domain, shareware.

A process in which a close corporation tries to force minority shareholders to sell their stock on terms favorable to the majority shareholders by preventing the minority shareholders from getting any profit from their investment. See also squeeze-out.
(1) Goods transported by a carrier such as a truck, train, aircraft, or ship. (2) The cost of transporting goods by a carrier. V. To transport goods by a carrier.
A business that consolidates small shipments of goods so they can all be sent as a single lot and assumes responsibility for their transport.
(1) A ship or airplane used to transport cargo. (2) A person or company in the business of transporting goods.
A list of questions frequently asked about a topic followed by their answers. ABBRV. FAQ.

Pursuit by police officers of a suspected felon fleeing the scene of the crime done immediately after the criminal act and without delay, which provides the officers with the common law right to cross state lines to make the arrest; also called hot pursuit.
A lawsuit brought by parties who agree on a particular settlement but need a judicial decision to make it binding; see also declaratory judgment.
Something given to an employee by an employer as part of compensation in addition to salary, such as health insurance; also called benefits.
A benefit that supplements an employee's pay, such as health insurance, profit-sharing plans, or recreational facilities.
A quick physical search of a person to feel for concealed weapons done by running hands over the body over clothing.
Unimportant; not serious; lacking in substance; presenting no legitimate legal question.
An appeal that has no merit, presents no justiciable question, and has no hope of success.
A cause of action that has no hope of success, brought primarily to annoy or embarrass the defendant.

term: fruit of the poisonous tree doctrine
definition: The principle that evidence obtained for a criminal trial through an illegal search or illegal interrogation of a witness is tainted and therefore inadmissible against the defendant because it violates the guarantee of due process.
The items acquired by a criminal through crime.

A situation that arises when a condition that was part of a contract does not occur, through no fault of either party, but the condition was the reason for one party entering the contract in the first place; can be cause for terminating the contract.
Flexible spending account.
Federal Trade Commission.
Fear, uncertainty, and doubt.
Someone who flees or hides in an attempt to evade capture.
A person who flees to another state or hides after committing a crime, hoping to avoid arrest.
A pardon removing all legal consequences from a conviction. pardon,

The constitutional requirement that all states must recognize the laws, judicial decisions, and public records of all other states and must give them the same degree of recognition that their own laws, judicial decisions, and public records receive.
An amount of money saved or intended for a particular purpose. V. To supply money for a particular purpose.
An amount of money deposited with the court either because the parties to a lawsuit both claim it, or to keep it in reserve to pay for potential liability.
An error committed by a trial court that harms the case enough to render the judgment void. error,
A debt that has a fund allocated for its payment.
Interchangeable; describes goods that are essentially identical to one another and that can replace one another, e.g., oil, grain, or money.
An estate for which a person might receive title or interest some time in the future. estate,
An interest in real or personal property that will begin at some specified time in the future.

An agreement to buy shares of a commodity at a fixed price but with delivery and payment occurring at an agreed-upon date in the future; if the price of the commodity rises during that period, the seller pays the buyer the difference between the agreed price and the current price, and if the price of the commodity drops, the buyer pays the seller the difference. fund, general 206

(1) An order issued by a judge prohibiting participants in a lawsuit from discussing the case publicly in order to protect the litigants' right to an impartial trial; gag orders may not usually be imposed on the press. (2) An order to restrain or silence a disruptive defendant in a criminal trial.
Profit; growth of wealth, property, or value. V.
A job or an occupation that earns money or generates profit; employment for pay that is suited to the abilities of the worker; also called gainful occupation.
To bet; to play games of chance for money. N. A risky enterprise that could turn out as either a profit or a loss.
Betting; playing games of chance for money.
Wild animals that can be hunted.
Federal and state laws that regulate the hunting of animals for sport or food.
A game in which the outcome is determined primarily by luck and chance rather than skill.
An archaic spelling of "jail."
To seize someone's money, property, or wages to settle a debt or claim.
The third party who is ordered by the court to surrender money or property owed to a debtor so that it can be used to pay the debtor's debts.
A court order that takes the property or money that a third party owes to a debtor (typically wages owed to the debtor by an employer) and gives it to a plaintiff creditor.
General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade.

The condition of being female or male, with emphasis on cultural implications of masculinity or femininity rather than biological and physical differences; the members of the female or male sex; see also sex.
A verdict that announces which party prevails; the ordinary kind of verdict. verdict,

term: General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade
definition: An international agreement that states guidelines and rules for international trade. ABBRV. GATT.
A bequest to be paid out of the general assets of an estate rather than a particular thing or money.
An election taking place in an entire state to elect offices that come open regularly.
An objection to a pleading for lack of substance.
Jurisdiction covering all types of cases that can be brought before a court.
A law that affects all people of a state or class.
A free software license used by software producers to ensure that all versions of software licensed by general public license will remain freely available. See also open source. ABBRV. GPL.
The character of a witness as portrayed by testimony of people who know him or her.
The health, peace, safety, and morality of the citizenry, promoted by Article I of the Constitution.

(1) The period of time it takes a person to be born, grow up, and have children of his or her own, usually between twenty and thirty years. (2) A set of family members all born around the same time and at the same stage of descent from a common ancestor.
A transfer of wealth that passes over one generation to go down to a younger one, e.g., a transfer from grandparents straight to grandchildren.
A trust created to facilitate a generation-skipping transfer.
A name for a product that has become synonymous with the product itself, such as "aspirin," which cannot be registered for trademark protection. trademark,
Laws that allow pharmacists to substitute generic drugs for equivalent brand-name drugs in order to save consumers money.
A name of an item that is so general and commonly used as to lack the distinctiveness necessary to make it eligible for trademark protection.

Four treaties created in Geneva, Switzerland, between 1863 and 1949 that set standards for how member nations should treat prisoners of war and civilians during wartime. See also jus in bello, laws of war.
The practice of modifying genital organs by cutting off portions of them; usually done to young girls.

The practice of killing a race or ethnic group; systematic eradication of an ethnic or national group by murder, forced birth control, removal of children from the group, and a general program of persecution.
Having to do with the combined effects of geography and politics.
To manipulate boundaries in dividing a state or territory into political divisions in order to secure an advantage for a certain party in elections. N. gerrymander.

Something given to someone else willingly and without expectation of payment; a voluntary and intentional giving of property by a competent donor that is delivered to and accepted by the recipient without exchange of consideration. gift,

A gift that is promised to someone by a donor who expects to die soon that will take effect if the donor does die as expected, but for which the donor retains the title while he or she is still living, and that becomes invalid if the donor recovers and does not die of the illness that he or she suffered from when the gift was made; also called a gift causa mortis.
A gift that occurs after an intermediate estate ends; e.g., if A gives B a life estate with a gift over to C, then C takes B's estate when B dies.
A tax imposed on gifts.
A company or enterprise currently conducting its ordinary business.
The value of a firm that is currently conducting business, which includes goodwill and other aspects that make it more valuable than its mere assets.
A good reason for doing or not doing something; a reason for one's behavior that is substantial enough to constitute a legal excuse, determined by the context of an individual case.
Sincerity, honesty, lack of deceit; a sincere intention to do what is promised.

Someone who voluntarily aids a person who is in imminent danger or has been injured without expectation of compensation; a good Samaritan is not held liable for injury to the victim unless he or she fails to use reasonable care and the victim is injured further by the efforts at assistance.

A title to property that is valid and can be sold without worry; a title free from doubts, encumbrances, and defects, and that is not the subject of litigation; also called merchantable title. Synonymous with clear title, marketable title.
Personal property, chattels; all property that is not land or buildings, including animals and crops and all other movable items. goods,
An intangible business asset composed of a good reputation with customers, suppliers, and the community at large, good employee morale, the ability to attract customers and clients, good
To control and regulate the affairs of an organization, institution, nation, state, or group of people.
A group or entity that controls the actions and policies of an institution, nation, state, group, or organization.
The body that controls, influences, and regulates the affairs of a nation, state, or community. ADJ. governmental.
An act done by the government or its agents that uses legislative, judicial, or administrative powers for the public good.
A doctrine limiting the federal government's power to tax states and the states' power to tax the federal government.
The elected executive leader of a state.
General public license.

A period between an official due date and the time when an actual penalty will be imposed for failure to pay or perform; in insurance, the time between the due date of a premium and the time when insurance will actually be cut off if payment is not made.
A crime that has different degrees and penalties depending on level of guilt; e.g. murder can come in first, second, or third degree. See also degree of crime.
Corrupt practices used by public officials to unlawfully take public money; money obtained by corruption.
The taking of property with a high monetary value, set by statute. larceny,
A group of people who are selected to investigate an alleged crime and indict suspected criminals. See also petit jury.
A clause in a new statute that exempts those who are already engaged in the enterprise or activity it regulates from the new requirements.
To give; to bestow something on someone; to agree (to a request). N. (1) A legal conveyance of property. (2) A sum of money given by the government or an organization for a particular purpose. grant,
A person who receives a grant.
A person who grants something.
Free; without charge.
(1) Done for free; given without expectation of payment. (2) Unjustified; done without good reason.
A promise made without consideration, making it unenforceable as a legal contract.
Money or other property given freely, usually in return for some service or favor.
The essence or most serious part of a complaint, cause of action, charge, etc.

An unofficial trade in something, especially of unissued shares of stock, controlled or scarce goods, or goods manufactured abroad that use U.S. trademarks without permission of the trademark holders.
The care exercised by a reasonable person in very important matters, or the care used by an extremely prudent person in a similar situation. care,
A registration card that allows a permanent resident alien to live and work in the United States.

A complaint; an allegation that someone has committed some injury or injustice that deserves recompense; in labor law, a complaint about working conditions filed by a worker or a union in order to seek relief.
Failure to use even the slightest care. negligence,
All income from all sources. See also negligence.
The total value of goods and services produced by a country in one year plus net income from foreign exports and investments.

A lease of vacant land with no buildings on it, usually rented in order to build structures such as office buildings or hotels on it; ground leases often run for many years and are renewable by the lessor.
Rent paid for the use of land, usually to build on it.
A security that is likely to increase in market value.
(1) A promise or formal statement that specific conditions are the case or will be met. (2) A person who receives a guarantee. V. guarantee.

(1) A clause in the Constitution in which the U.S. government guarantees each state a republican form of government and promises to protect each of the states against invasion and against domestic violence. (2) A clause in a contract in which one party promises to pay someone else's obligations if necessary.
A person or organization that guarantees something; one who makes a guarantee.
A person who looks after the legal and financial affairs or takes care of someone who is unable to look after his or her own because of age or disability; see also ad litem, ward.
A guardian chosen by a child who lacks parents. guardian,
Someone who receives hospitality; someone invited into another person's home for entertainment or driven by another person in a car without paying for the ride.

A statute that absolves a driver from liability injury to a passenger in the car if the injury was the result of ordinary negligence, requiring that the driver exhibit gross or willful negligence, reckless disregard, or some other higher level of carelessness to be liable; many states have repealed these statutes.
(1) The condition of having committed a crime or wrongful act. (2) A feeling of remorse after committing a wrongful act or failing in some obligation.
(1) Having committed a crime; culpable; having been determined by a jury to have committed a specific crime. (2) Remorseful; see also innocent.
A voluntary admission of having committed a crime made to a court by a criminal defendant, punishable in the same way as a guilty verdict after trial.

(Latin) You have the body; a writ that institutes a court proceeding to determine whether a criminal defendant has been lawfully imprisoned, or to test the constitutionality of a conviction; also used in cases of child custody and deportation.
Referring to a building or dwelling, of good enough quality to be lived in or occupied without serious inconvenience or danger to health and safety. N. habitability.
Customary, usual, regular.
The practice of frequently drinking to the point of intoxication.
A person who regularly commits crimes; also called a habitual criminal. Synonymous with recidivist.

To break into a closed computer network by using software or clever programming. N. A quick fix to a computer programming problem; a modification to a program that allows the user to enter an area of the program or network that was previously closed to the user.
To drag forcibly; to summon forcefully into court.
A temporary residence for people who have left an institution, such as a prison or mental hospital, to help them readjust to life in society.
Writing done by hand with a hand-held implement such as a pen or pencil; the unique writing produced by a particular person and identifiable as his or her production.
A form of capital punishment in which the convicted person has a rope placed around his or her neck and is then hung until dead. V. hang.

term: Happening by chance rather than design
definition: fortuity.

(1) To pressure, intimidate, or attack repeatedly; to annoy; to insult or abuse verbally. (2) To bring a criminal prosecution against someone without a reasonable expectation of conviction. N. harassment. See also harassment, sexual. harassment,
(1) To provide shelter to someone. (2) To hide or take in a criminal or alien,
A legal case in which the judicial decision accommodates the extreme hardship faced by one party by deviating from strict legal principles.
To conceal; to make difficult to understand.
diligent. diligence,
Suffering, privation, difficulty, oppression. See unnecessary hardship.
Computer equipment; the physical devices that make up a computer system. See also software.
An error committed by a trial court that did not harm the rights of the party appealing the judgment and for which an appellate court will not reverse the trial court's judgment. error,

An error made by an appellate court during a trial that is usually trivial or academic, does not prejudice the rights of the party affected by it, and does not necessitate the reversal of the judgment.

term: having enough money to cover one's expenses
definition: solvency. See also insolvent.
High deductible health plan.

term: he or she forfeits the bail
definition: To furnish money or property to get someone released from prison.
(1) A person who lives with and supports one or more people living in the same home, and generally manages household affairs. (2) An unmarried taxpayer who houses and supports one or more dependents.
A short summary of a case and its legal holdings placed at the beginning of the case report.
Insurance that covers illness, injury, and medical care for the insured. insurance,

term: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
definition: A federal law enacted in 1996 that allows many workers to maintain health insurance coverage when they lose or change jobs and that protects the privacy of health data. ABBRV. HIPAA.

A form of health insurance consisting of a group of physicians, hospitals, and other health care providers that join together to provide health care services for people who pay a fee to the group. ABBRV. HMO.
A health insurance plan; a benefits plan that provides medical care through a predetermined network of doctors, clinics, and hospitals. See also insurance.

A savings account available to people who participate in a high deductible health plan that allows them to save pretax earnings to pay for qualified medical expenses and carry over their savings from year to year. ABBRV. HSA.
The prevention and treatment of diseases and injuries by medical professionals and institutions.
soundness.

A legal proceeding, usually less formal than a trial, in which the parties to a case are given an opportunity to present evidence and testimony to a judge or other official who determines the facts and makes a decision based on the evidence presented.
A hearing in which a matter that has already gone through trial is presented again as if for the first time, starting over from the beginning.
A report made by a witness of something that another person said or communicated nonverbally, usually not admissible as evidence.
The mental and emotional state caused by sudden anger, hatred, terror, or extreme excitement that provokes someone to commit a crime.
To protect an investment by making a compensatory or counterbalancing transaction. N. hedging.
A mutual fund or partnership of investors that uses hedging techniques such as arbitrage, futures contracts, and selling short in an attempt to maximize profits.
A person legally entitled to inherit an estate if its owner dies without a will. heir,
A person who has a legal right to inherit an estate if he or she outlives its owner, and whose right to the property cannot be defeated.
An heir who is the natural child or direct lineal descendant of the decedent.
A person who would inherit a property if the owner were to die immediately, but whose right could be defeated if a nearer heir is born first.
A female heir.
A valuable object that is passed down through generations of a family.
A phrase used in conveying an estate in fee simple, used to define and limit the class of people who can inherit the estate.
A thing that can be inherited.
Transmittable through or based on inheritance.
Inheritance; the passing of estates through the laws of descent and distribution.
Before the present time.
Within or further on in the document.
Concealed from view.
An asset recorded on a company's books at much less than its market value.
A defect in an object or property that is not immediately apparent on inspection and for which a buyer has the right to revoke an acceptance to purchase.
A tax built into the price of an item, usually incurred at some point in manufacturing or distribution.

A health insurance plan that attempts to reduce costs by imposing a high deductible on medical expenses, on the reasoning that if consumers must pay for most medical care themselves they will avoid unnecessary care and shop for the lowest prices when they do seek treatment. ABBRV. hdhp. See also health savings account.
Leader or head.
To commandeer a vehicle such as an airplane, bus, or ship, while in transit, in order to take it to a different destination, to steal its cargo, or to take hostages. N. hijacking.
Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act.
To give someone a job; to take someone on as an employee. N. A person who has been given a job.

Describes an automobile accident in which the driver of one of the vehicles involved immediately leaves the scene without identifying himself or herself or providing statutorily required information to the police or other drivers and passengers.
Health maintenance organization.
To acquire and keep goods or money beyond what is needed,
An activity engaged in for pleasure during leisure time, not done for profit.
A loss incurred from a hobby, usually not deductible.
To assume the liability for a situation, thereby absolving another party of any responsibility for it. See also indemnify, save harmless.
A person with legal possession of a document of title, promissory note, check, or other instrument, and who is entitled to receive payment on it.
A bona fide purchaser who acquires an instrument in good faith, for value, and without notice of any claim against the instrument.
(1) A court's ruling on an issue presented at trial; a legal principle produced by a court in deciding a case. (2) Property or stocks owned by someone.
A company that exists solely to own the stock of or manage the affairs of another company.
The period of time an asset must be held before its sale or exchange will result in a capital gain or loss.
A lawsuit without a legal basis instituted to delay or obstruct some occurrence.
A will written by hand by a testator, signed by the testator, and not witnessed; also called a holographic will.
A room or area in a dwelling set aside as a professional workplace.
A loan in which a lender gives money to a borrower in exchange for a lien on the borrower's house, using the borrower's equity in his or her house as collateral.
The port where a ship or vessel is registered, or the port nearest to the residence of its owner or manager.
A doctrine in maritime law holding that a vessel engaged in foreign and interstate commerce is subject to property tax only at its home port.

A means of apportioning power between state and local governments that allows local entities a certain degree of independent self-government, allowing them to pass laws without first getting permission from the state.
The U.S. government's collective efforts to keep the territory of the United States free from hazards, including natural disasters such as hurricanes and man-made disasters such as terrorist attacks.
The residence of a family, including house, land, and outbuildings. homestead,
A doctrine that allows a homeowner to designate his or her home as a homestead and thereby exempt it from claims by creditors.
The killing of one human being by another. homicide,
To accept a check, note, credit card, or other form of payment. V. A title of courtesy given to a judge, as in the phrase "Your Honor" or the adjective "Honorable."
A declaration by the government that a soldier is leaving military service with a good record.

A payment of money or a valuable item rendered to a professional in exchange for a service for which a fee is traditionally or legally not paid; a gift given instead of pay as consideration for services.
Conferred as an honor or reward without the usual requirements or responsibilities.
Price fixing done by businesses selling the same items, i.e., competitors. price fixing,
A short treatise that summarizes the fundamental principles of a branch of law; a primer.
General principles of law that are widely known and not questioned; see also black letter law.
A person held by a criminal or an enemy who threatens to kill or harm the hostage if demands are not met or promises are broken. See also kidnap.
Antagonistic; acting like an enemy.
Harassment that is determined by a pattern of sexual behavior by supervisors or fellow employees that creates an environment that the victim finds intolerable. harassment,
Occupying and claiming ownership of a property without permission of the owner.
A witness who is so obviously antagonistic to the party who has called him or her that the party is allowed to
(1) A building or structure used as a dwelling. (2) A legislative body. V. To provide a home for someone.
The British legislative house that represents the common people, all subjects except the nobility and royalty.
The lower house of the British Parliament, consisting of representatives from all the common people of the realm.
The upper house of the British Parliament, consisting of representatives from the nobility.
A building in which prostitutes work.
The larger of the two legislative bodies that make up Congress, consisting of elected representatives from each state whose number is set by population; often called the House.
A church or other building used for religious services.
Health savings account.
The management of personnel and issues surrounding employees, such as hiring, firing, pay, discipline, and benefits.
The basic rights to which all humans are generally considered to be entitled, which can include life, liberty, freedom of speech, freedom of religion, due process, equal rights, and dignity.
Working to promote the welfare and preserve the dignity of human beings.
A jury that cannot agree on a verdict.
Privilege covering spouses, who are not required to reveal confidential communications between them while married.
A state of consciousness in which the subject loses the power to control his or her actions and becomes very sensitive to suggestion. V. hypnotize.
A person who hypnotizes others.
To pledge money or property for a particular purpose, often as security for a debt.
A supposition; a proposed explanation for an occurrence based on available evidence, serving as the beginning of further exploration of the question. See also theory.
Based on assumptions but not necessarily true.
A question in which the questioner asks someone to offer an opinion based on the assumption that certain conditions are true. hung jury 230
(Latin) Ibidem. ibidem. (Latin) In the same place; used to cite a source that has already been cited in full in the preceding footnote. ABBRV. ibid.
Internet Corporation for Assigned Names and Numbers.
Interstate Commerce Commission.
(Latin) Idem. idem. (Latin) The same; used when citing a text that is part of the source that has just been cited in the immediately preceding reference. ABBRV. id.
The state of being unaware of something;
Forbidden by law or not according to law; unlawful.
A foreign national present in a country without legal permission to be there.
Evidence acquired by violating a person's constitutional protection against illegal searches and seizures; evidence obtained without a warrant or probable cause.
(1) Not authorized by law; illegal. (2) Born to parents not married to one another at the time of conception.
Illegal; against rules or custom.
Not real; deceptive; having a false appearance.
A promise with such vague terms that it actually promises nothing because the person making it is allowed to
irrelevance, irrelevancy.
Evidence that has no real bearing on the issue in question.
A person who moves to a foreign country to live there.
To move to a foreign country with the intention of living there permanently. N. immigration.
The federal agency that registers immigrants and oversees the process of making them into citizens. See also naturalize. ABBRV. INS.
Against accepted moral standards;
Conduct that is willful, flagrant, or shameless, and in disregard of the moral opinions of respectable members of the community.
Exemption or protection from something such as prosecution, duty, or penalty. immunity,
To list or enroll the jurors who have been selected for a trial.
Fair, unbiased; not favoring one side over another.
To accuse a public official of misconduct; to question the validity of something, such as a judgment, or the integrity of a witness.
A criminal procedure in which a public official is charged with misconduct.
Not pertinent;
A conclusion that can be drawn from information but that is not expressly stated; a likely consequence.
A warranty implied by the seller's actions or words.
Power given to an agent by a principal that is not expressly stated but that can be assumed as a necessary part of the agent's job.
Powers not expressly stated or enumerated but that can be implied as necessary to do a particular job, sometimes found by the Supreme Court in its interpretation of the Constitution.
To suggest strongly that something is true without stating it directly. See also infer.
To bring goods into a country for sale. N. Goods brought from another country for sale. See also export.
The act of importing goods.
A person who imports goods. See also customs.
A situation that makes it impossible to do something; circumstances that make it impossible to perform a contract,
Someone who pretends to be someone else with the intention of deceiving others, usually to gain some benefit.
To seize and take legal custody of something, such as a vehicle; to capture animals and shut them in an enclosure.
So inconvenient or difficult as to be essentially impossible. N. impracticability.
(Latin) Let it be printed; a license to print and publish a book.
To put in prison; to keep someone restricted to a place that essentially functions as a prison; to confine; to deprive of liberty.
See incarcerate.
To make something better.
A change that makes something better; an addition or change to buildings or land that goes beyond mere repairs and enhances its value, utility, or attractiveness.
To attribute; to assign someone responsibility for the act of another person over whom the first person exercises control.
Value acquired by a taxpayer through use of his or her own property, or by performing work services for himself or herself, and not included in gross income. income,
Knowledge that is attributed to a person because he or she had a duty to know it and it was available. in absentia. ADV. (Latin) In absence; not physically present at an event.
Not able to be admitted under the rules of evidence.

term: inadvertently also prohibits constitutionally protected activities
definition: overbreadth. See also void, vague.
Not able to be taken away or given up without consent of the possessor.
A property interest that cannot be sold or transferred.

Rights such as freedom of speech, equal protection of the laws, and due process that cannot be taken away from or given up by anyone without his or her consent. in camera. ADV. (Latin) In chambers; done in a judge's private chambers.
Inability to do or understand something; lack of physical, mental, or legal ability to do a particular task.
To put in jail or prison; to imprison. N. incarceration.
Sexual contact between people who are so closely related to one another by blood that they are prohibited by law from marrying. in chief. ADJ. Main; primary; principal.
Not yet fully developed; incomplete. See also choate.
A crime that could constitute the initial stages of another crime; e.g., assault could be an inchoate form of battery.
A potential future interest in property.
(1) An event. (2) Something that is inseparably connected with something else. ADJ. Attached to something in a subordinate way; dependent on something.
Partial or complete ownership or control of a life insurance policy.
Accompanying something else, usually in a subordinate way; occurring in connection with something else by chance or as a consequence.
A person who is indirectly benefited by a contract formed between other people.
Powers that are a necessary accompaniment to powers expressly granted.
A piece of land surrounded by a fence, wall, or other barrier; also called an enclosure.
Money received as compensation for work, through investments, or as profits from a business. income,

term: income in respect of a decedent
definition: Income earned by a taxpayer but received by his or her heirs or personal representatives after his or her death.
A statement of financial gains and losses over a twelve-month period; also called earnings report, operating statement, profit and loss statement.
Tax levied on a person's income by the federal government and some state governments.
The state of being incompetent.
Lacking the skills or capability necessary to do something;
(1) Self-contradictory; not staying the same; adopting two or more mutually exclusive viewpoints simultaneously. (2) Not in keeping with one's own beliefs. See also prior inconsistent statement.
(1) To combine something into something else as part of one whole. (2) To create a corporation. N. incorporation.
Having no physical or material existence; intangible.
Property that exists as a legal right without physical substance, such as a copyright.
An increase, usually part of a scale of regular increases.
To suggest or charge that someone is guilty of a crime. N.
Evidence that helps establish the guilt of an accused person. See also self-incrimination.
incriminating.
Accuse; blame.
Helping to establish guilt or incriminate a criminal defendant.
A person who currently holds a particular office. ADJ. Necessary; required as part of duty or responsibility.
Offensive, vulgar, against modesty or proper behavior,
Lewdly exposing private parts of the body in a public place. See also obscene.
Not able to be defeated, annulled, or revoked; usually describes an estate or a right that cannot be revoked.

A sentence that has a prescribed range of punishments set by law, for which the court and other officials have some discretion in sentencing and that may be ended early at the discretion of the parole board; also called indeterminate sentence. sentence,
A dead person's lack of living children or descendants that can occur at any time after the decedent's death, however far in the future; see also failure of issue.
To pay or reimburse someone for a loss or injury; to take on legal responsibility for someone else's actions; to insure.
Money given as compensation or reimbursement for a loss or injury; security against legal responsibility for one's own actions; the benefit provided by an insurance policy.
A promise to take on another person's liability or compensate him or her if and when that liability arises.
Compensation given to a person when he or she suffers a particular loss. See also insure, subrogation.

(1) A formal agreement conveying real estate from one party to another and binding both parties with obligations. (2) An agreement issuing corporate bonds and debentures, often between a corporation and an indenture trustee, who holds title to the trust property and carries out the terms of the agreement. (3) Historically, a legal contract made into several copies with the
A person who does a job for another person independently, using his or her own methods and is not under the control of the employer in regard to how the work is accomplished.
A person or group of people appointed to investigate accusations of criminal conduct by a high-level public official.

(1) An alphabetical list of entries or topics in a book or series, including the pages on which they appear. (2) A statistical method of tracking fluctuations in the value of stocks, mortgages, inflation, the economy, etc., over time.
A mutual fund that chooses its stocks according to a stock market index number.
Tying wages, mortgages, or other financial issues to inflation in order to keep values relatively constant.
For the purposes of many U.S. laws and treaties, an indigenous American; a Native American or American Indian. Indian Affairs,
A commission that hears claims brought against the federal government by Indian tribes or other groups of American Indians.
Public land set aside for the use of American Indians, supervised by the federal government.
A right granted to American Indian tribes by the federal government to use and occupy certain territories that they have occupied for a long time, though without any right of ownership.
A distinct group of American Indians of the same or similar race, occupying a specific territory, and under the same leadership.
Indications, signs; circumstances indicating that something is probably true; synonymous with circumstantial evidence.
A document showing title to a piece of property.
To formally charge someone with a crime.
A crime that can be prosecuted by indictment, usually a serious crime.
A person who has been indicted.
A formal, written accusation presented by a grand jury to the court charging someone with a specified crime.
recklessness.

An ethnic group that was present in a nation before the current population was created by colonization from a foreign nation, which generally maintains a culture, language, and social organization distinct from that of the current surrounding population.
Poor; poverty-stricken;
A criminal defendant who lacks the financial resources to pay for a lawyer to defend himor herself.
In divorce law, abusive or cruel treatment directed at the mind and emotions rather than the body, such as vulgarity, harsh criticism, ridicule, neglect, or abusive language.
Circuitous or roundabout; not going straight from one point to another; not directly caused by something.
Evidence that makes a hypothesis appear plausible but does not actually prove it. See also circumstantial evidence, direct evidence.
Necessary; essential.
Evidence that is absolutely necessary to prove a particular fact.
A party who has such an important interest in a lawsuit that the judgment cannot be issued without his or her presence; see also joinder.
A tax-advantaged personal retirement account that an individual acquires and funds himself or herself,
To endorse; to sign the back of a check or negotiable instrument in order to transfer its payment to someone else. N. indorsement.
The person to whom a check or instrument is transferred by indorsement. indorsement,
The person who signs the check or instrument to transfer it to another.
To cause; to bring about; to influence or persuade.
The benefit that persuades a party to enter a contract; the motive that causes someone to commit a crime.
Relations between workers and their employer on issues of compensation, safety, hiring, etc.
Commercial activity conducted for profit; economic activity that uses a great deal of labor, capital, and raw materials for the manufacture of goods. ADJ. industrial.
To make intoxicated or drunk.
A person under the influence of too much alcohol.
Not legally allowed to hold a position or conduct an activity;
Well-known for some misdeed or bad reputation; notorious;
Historically, a crime that renders its perpetrator infamous; under the modern view, a crime punishable by death or imprisonment in a state penal institution for more than one year.
Punishment by imprisonment or by hard labor.
Childhood; minority; the period before which a person reaches legal majority.
A person below the age of legal majority, or before marriage. See also emancipate.
Killing a baby soon after birth.
To deduce or conclude through a process of reasoning based on facts and logic. N. inference. See also imply.
A court whose decisions are subject to review by an appellate court; see also trial court.
Weak; sickly.

Weakness or disability, especially caused by age or disease; in insurance, a disease or illness that substantially impairs a person's health, and if revealed will discourage an insurer from issuing that person a life or health insurance policy.
Unofficial; not drafted or executed in the proper legal form. N. informality.

A contract that is valid without being executed formally, such as an oral contract. in forma pauperis. ADV. (Latin) In the form of a pauper; the right given an indigent person to sue without having to pay court fees or costs.

(1) A trial or hearing less formal than a regular trial, such as a proceeding in a small claims court. (2) In probate court, a proceeding that admits a will to probate without first providing notice to all interested parties.

A written document filed with the court accusing someone of a crime that serves essentially the same function as an indictment but without a grand jury, informing the accused of the charge against him or her.

A legal term used to describe testimony that is not based on a witness's firsthand knowledge but that the witness nevertheless sincerely believes to be true, and that is based on a reasonable, good faith effort to know the truth.
The field of study and work that concerns itself with computers, networks, and the electronic transmission of information. ABBRV. IT.

Consent given after learning and understanding all relevant facts needed to make an intelligent decision, required in situations where someone is giving up rights, as when being arrested, or in potentially risky situations, such as surgery.
A person who confidentially gives information about a crime to police officers; also called an informant.
The government's right to keep the identity of an informer secret from the defendant about whom the informer has provided information.
(Latin) Below; used to refer to information further on in a document. See also supra.
Violation of a law, agreement, or duty.

To encroach or trespass on something; to violate the terms of a contract, right, or law; to violate a copyright, patent, or trademark by using it without permission or payment to the owner. N. infringement.
Entrance; the act of entering or the right of entrance; opposite of egress.
To live in a place; to dwell, to reside.
Someone who lives in a place.
Existing as a permanent, essential, and intrinsic part of something.
A defect built into an object that exists regardless of how it is used.
Powers and authority that are an intrinsic part of an office or position and that exist without being expressly granted.
A right that a person has simply by virtue of being a person and that is not granted from some outside source.
To receive property or money as an heir after the owner's death; technically refers to receiving property by the rules of
Tax imposed on someone who receives property and money through inheritance.
Cruel, brutal, lacking normal human compassion.
Treatment that is so physically or mentally cruel or brutal as to endanger the health or life of its victim.
The first set of shares a new company sells on a public stock exchange, usually in an effort to raise money from investors in order to enlarge the company. ABBRV. IPO.
A process in which citizens file a petition proposing a new piece of legislation and submit it to a vote by the legislature or the electorate. See also referendum.
A court order prohibiting someone from doing a specified act in order to prevent future injury. ADJ. injunctive. injunction,
To harm or damage a person or property.
Harm or damage; damage that comes from the violation of a legal right. injury,

An absence of fairness; an unfair occurrence; a mistake made by a court that results in unfair and unjust treatment. in kind. ADJ. Of the same kind, category, or type; used to describe the act of exchanging one thing for another thing of the same or similar type. ADV. in kind. in loco parentis. ADJ. (Latin) In the place of a parent; describes a situation in which someone (e.g., a teacher or camp counselor) supervising a minor temporarily assumes the legal obligations normally held by a parent or guardian. ADV. in loco parentis.
A person confined to a prison, hospital, or other institution.
Not guilty; in good faith; without intention of committing a wrong or knowledge that a wrong is being committed. N. innocence.
To create something new. N. innovation.
A suggestive remark or hint; in a libel action, the part of the pleading in which the plaintiff explains the meaning of the allegedly libelous words. in perpetuity. ADJ. Forever;
Immigration and Naturalization Services.
Madness or mental illness; the condition of being mentally ill to the point that one's perceptions and behavior are seriously impaired and one is not responsible for one's actions. ADJ. insane.

A defense to criminal prosecution based on the claim that the defendant cannot be guilty because he or she lacked the ability to behave correctly and the capacity to understand that his or her act was criminal.

In securities and corporations, someone who has access to information about a business that is not available to the general public, such as corporate directors, officers, and major stockholders; insiders are not allowed to buy and sell stocks in such a way as to take advantage of their privileged position and use it for personal gain.
Information about a corporation that is available only to insiders and not revealed to the general public.
The illegal buying and selling of stock based on information available only to insiders.
To examine closely, looking for defects and quality, often done to make sure the object meets some official standard; to scrutinize. N. inspection.
A process done during discovery in which a party examines and copies documents held by the opposing party that are relevant to the lawsuit.

(1) An official charged with issuing permits and inspecting objects or places, such as buildings or restaurants, to ensure that they adhere to legal standards. (2) In some jurisdictions, a police officer.
A high-ranking administrative official in charge of monitoring operations and finding and eradicating fraud and waste.
A note that is paid in several installments over a period of time. note,
A contract that divides its obligation, such as delivery of goods or payment, into portions that are performed periodically over a specified period of time.
A contract in which a consumer purchases an item and receives it at once but pays for it over a period of time in installments.
Current, present.
The case currently under consideration.
To begin, establish, or inaugurate; to commence legal proceedings. N. (1) An organization with a particular purpose, often an authority in a particular field. (2) A commentary on legal principles.

(1) The act of starting or establishing something; the act of beginning a legal action. (2) An organization with a professional, charitable, educational, or similar purpose. (3) An organization or place that houses people with special needs, such as mental patients or prisoners.
To teach; to provide information about something; to direct or command.
Directions given by a judge to a jury, telling them how to apply the law to the facts of the case; see also charge.
A formal written document; a writing that formally expresses some legal agreement, such as a contract, lease, will, deed, or bond. See also negotiable instrument.
Inadequate;
The condition of not having enough evidence to support a claim at trial, determined by the court and resulting in a directed verdict for the defendant.
The condition of having less money in a bank account than has been drawn on that account.
On an island; having to do with islands; isolated; provincial.
Able to be insured.

A connection or relationship with a person or thing that is substantial enough to bring a real benefit or advantage to the person who wants to insure it, generally because the person or thing provides him or her with money or some other valuable thing, thus justifying the need or desire for insurance coverage.

A contract in which one party, the insurer, agrees to compensate the other party, the insured, for specified losses or damage to property or people in exchange for consideration, usually the payment of a premium.
A person who investigates and settles insurance claims.
A person who represents an insurance company, selling policies on its behalf.
A public official who supervises the insurance business within a state.

Legal work done by a law firm that specializes in defending claims brought against or covered by an insurance company, usually with a special fee arrangement giving the insurance company discounted rates for high volume work.
A contract of insurance.
To promise to compensate someone for the loss of or damage to someone or something in exchange for payments of money; to assume the risk of loss or damage; to underwrite; see also indemnify.
A person covered by an insurance policy.
A company or individual that insures people or things; an underwriter.
A person who rises up against the government or authorities.
A violent uprising against the government or authority; a rebellion by citizens against the government.
Property with no physical existence, such as a copyright. property,
Property that has no physical substance but that nevertheless exists as a right, such as a patent, copyright, or goodwill; also called intangibles.
(1) To combine two or more things into one whole. (2) To desegregate.
A written contract that contains the entire and final agreement of the parties, and to which oral evidence cannot be admitted; also called an integration.

term: intended to cause erotic stimulation rather than education
definition: pornographic. See also obscene, prurient interest.

Plan, resolve, or intention; desire to bring about a particular result; the state of mind of a person who wants to do a certain act and accomplish a particular result. V. intend. See also motive. intent,
A plan, desire, or aim; something that is intended.
Deliberate; done consciously and purposely with an awareness of probable consequences; not accidental.
A tort committed deliberately with an express desire to harm.
(Latin) Among other things; used to avoid listing all the details of a statute or other document while noting that they exist.
